text: Chaos of the Senses is a novel by the novelist, Ahlam Mostaghanmi published in 1997 by Hachette Antoine publishing house. The novel was translated by Nancy Roberts.

Alfred Lubbock (31 October 1845 – 17 July 1916) was an English insurance underwriter and banker. He is best known as an amateur cricketer who played first-class cricket for a variety of sides including Kent County Cricket Club and the Marylebone Cricket Club between 1863 and 1875. He was considered to be one of the best batsman of his era, comparable to WG Grace, and also played association football, playing for Old Etonians in the 1875 FA Cup Final.

Beta Delphini (β Delphini, abbreviated Beta Del, β Del) is a binary star in the constellation of Delphinus. It is the brightest star in Delphinus.

The two components of the system are designated Beta Delphini A (officially named Rotanev /ˈroʊtənɛv/, which is historically the name of the system) and B.

Kafiristan, meaning "land of the infidels" in Persian, is a historical region located in present-day northeastern Afghanistan and northwestern Pakistan. The name derives from the Arabic term *kāfir*, meaning "non-believer," and historically referred to the region's inhabitants, who practiced a form of polytheism before their forced conversion to Islam in 1895–96 under Emir Abdur Rahman Khan of Afghanistan. Following their conversion, the region was renamed Nuristan, meaning "land of the enlightened."

Kafiristan was historically part of the ancient Kapisa kingdom, which flourished under Buddhist rule until the decline of Buddhism in the region. The area became isolated and retained its distinct culture and religion until the late 19th century. European explorers, including Bento de Góis, Alexander Gardner, and George Scott Robertson, documented the region and its people before their conversion.

The region was known for its steep valleys, intricate wood carvings, and unique cultural practices. After its conquest, many artifacts were destroyed or repurposed, with some preserved in museums. A small group of Kafirs fled to Chitral, where the Kalasha people, descendants of the "Black Kafirs," continue to practice their traditional beliefs. Today, the region is part of Afghanistan's Nuristan Province and Pakistan's Chitral District, with the Kalasha culture surviving in isolated valleys.
Lawton is a small city in Ramsey County, North Dakota, United States. Founded in 1902, it covers 0.99 square miles and had a population of 15 in 2020.

The Western Sahara peace process refers to ongoing efforts to resolve the conflict between the Polisario Front, representing the Sahrawi people, and Morocco over the disputed territory of Western Sahara. The conflict, rooted in the decolonization of Spanish Sahara in 1975, has involved armed conflict, ceasefires, and unarmed civil campaigns. Despite multiple peace initiatives, the region remains contested, with Morocco controlling most of the territory.

The COVID-19 pandemic in Tunisia is part of the ongoing pandemic of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) caused by severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2). The disease was confirmed to have reached Tunisia on 2 March 2020.

The A5 motorway (Greek: Αυτοκινητόδρομος 5), also known as the Ionia Odos, is a major highway in Greece that spans 196 kilometers (122 miles), connecting Ioannina in the north to Rio in the south. It forms part of the European routes E55 and E951 and traverses the regions of Epirus, West Greece, and the Peloponnese, serving major cities such as Ioannina, Arta, Agrinio, and Patras. The motorway includes significant infrastructure, such as the Rio–Antirrio Bridge, the world's longest fully suspended cable-stayed bridge, inaugurated in 2004.

Construction of the A5 has been carried out in phases, with key segments like the Agrinio and Arta bypasses completed between 2003 and 2017. The motorway's development faced delays due to financial, legal, and environmental challenges but was largely completed by 2017. The northern section is managed by the Nea Odos consortium, while the southern Patras bypass is overseen by the Olympia Odos consortium. The A5 has significantly improved transportation in western Greece, reducing travel times and enhancing connectivity between regions.

Han Lao Da (born Ann Jong Juan on 22 October 1947) is a Singaporean playwright, xiangsheng artist, and principal of the Han Language Centre. Renowned for promoting xiangsheng in Singapore, he has written numerous scripts, published collections, and contributed to Singapore's drama and cultural scene.

The Gao Empire preceded the Songhai Empire in the region of the Middle Niger. It owes its name to the town of Gao located at the eastern Niger bend. In the ninth century CE, it was considered to be the most powerful West African kingdom.

Gao was founded in the 7th century. Because of its placement along the Niger River, it was a profitable location for fisherman to settle. Gao is one of the oldest trading centers in western Africa. Because of the placement of Gao, Gao became the capital for the Songhai (or Songhay) Empire in the early 11th century. Gao was able to flourish as the capital of the Songhai Empire, most notably via the trans-Saharan trade of gold, copper, slaves, and salt. Gao was annexed by the rulers of the kingdom of Mali in 1325, but Songhai would eventually retain control of it 40 years later.

There are no surviving indigenous written records that date from before the middle of the 17th century. Our knowledge of the early history of the town relies on the writings of external Arabic geographers living in Morocco, Egypt and Andalusia, who never visited the region.

"Heartache Tonight" is a song by the Eagles, released on September 18, 1979, as the lead single from their album *The Long Run*. Written by Don Henley, Glenn Frey, Bob Seger, and J.D. Souther, the song originated from a jam session and features a lively, blues rock style. It won the 1979 Grammy Award for Best Rock Performance by a Duo or Group with Vocal. The song was later covered by artists such as Conway Twitty, whose 1983 version reached No. 6 on the Billboard Hot Country Singles chart.

The Most Eminent Order of the Indian Empire was an order of chivalry established by Queen Victoria in 1878 to honor British and Indian officials who served in British India. Initially comprising a single class, the order expanded to three classes—Knight Grand Commander (GCIE), Knight Commander (KCIE), and Companion (CIE)—by 1887. It was intended as a less exclusive counterpart to the Order of the Star of India. Membership included British officials, Indian princely rulers, and other notable figures, with women generally excluded except for female rulers.

Appointments ceased after Indian independence in 1947, and the order became dormant in 2010. The British monarch remains its sovereign. Members were entitled to various privileges, including heraldic honors, and the order's insignia reflected respect for India's non-Christian traditions. Notable appointees included rulers of Bhutan and other dignitaries.

Muthoot Microfin Limited, the microfinance arm of Muthoot Pappachan Group, is an Indian microfinance institution focused on providing microloans to women entrepreneurs with a focus on rural regions of India.
St John's Rectory is at 3 Vicar's Lane, Chester, Cheshire, England. It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II* listed building.

The Braille pattern dots-1456 (⠹) is a 6-dot braille cell with both top, the middle right, and bottom right dots raised, or an 8-dot braille cell with both top, the upper-middle right, and lower-middle right dots raised. It is represented by the Unicode code point U+2839, and in Braille ASCII with a question mark:?.

The Meaning of Life is an Irish television programme, broadcast on RTÉ One. Presented by Gay Byrne, each edition involves the veteran broadcaster interviewing a prominent public figure.

Interviews with former Taoiseach Bertie Ahern and actors Gabriel Byrne and Brenda Fricker during the second series attracted media attention. Ahern spoke of his religious habits, Byrne and Fricker of being sexually abused as children.
Lawton is a city in Ramsey County, North Dakota, United States. The population was 15 at the 2020 census. Lawton was founded in 1902.
Frank S Cherry (circa 1875-1963) was the founder and leader of one of the early Hebrew Israelite groups in the United States.

Titus Lucretius Carus (/ˈtaɪtəs luːˈkriːʃəs/ TY-təs loo-KREE-shəs, IPA-la; c. 99 – c. 55 BC) was a Roman poet and philosopher. His only known work is the philosophical poem De rerum natura, a didactic work about the tenets and philosophy of Epicureanism, and which usually is translated into English as On the Nature of Things and somewhat less often as On the nature of the universe. Lucretius has been credited with originating the concept of the three-age system that was formalised in 1836 by C. J. Thomsen.

Very little is known about Lucretius's life; the only certainty is that he was either a friend or client of Gaius Memmius, to whom the poem was addressed and dedicated.

De rerum natura was a considerable influence on the Augustan poets, particularly Virgil (in his Aeneid and Georgics, and to a lesser extent on the Eclogues) and Horace. The work was almost lost during the Middle Ages, but was rediscovered in 1417 in a monastery in Germany by Poggio Bracciolini and it played an important role both in the development of atomism (Lucretius was an important influence on Pierre Gassendi) and the efforts of various figures of the Enlightenment era to construct a new Christian humanism. Lucretius's scientific poem On the Nature of Things (c. 60 BC) has a remarkable description of Brownian motion of dust particles in verses 113–140 from Book II. He uses this as a proof of the existence of atoms.

Curtis Knight and the Squires was an R&B band active in the 1960s, notable for featuring Jimi Hendrix as a member from 1965 to 1966. The group released several recordings, including Hendrix's earliest compositions, such as "Hornet's Nest" and "Knock Yourself Out." Their work has been revisited in later releases, including the 2015 album *You Can't Use My Name: The RSVP/PPX Sessions*. Hendrix's time with the band was marked by a controversial contract with PPX Studios, which later caused legal issues.

The Leader of Alderney is the civil leader of Alderney. Alderney is a dependency of the Bailiwick of Guernsey. Its leader has traditionally been appointed by the British Crown and has been known by various titles including Lord of Alderney, Governor of Alderney, and the current President of the States of Alderney. The President of the States of Alderney is directly elected every four years and there is no constitutional limit to the number of terms served. The current president, William Tate has held the post since 2019.

MV *Empire Day* was a British CAM ship and cargo vessel built in 1941 by William Doxford & Sons Ltd in Sunderland for the Ministry of War Transport. Initially armed with a Hawker Sea Hurricane, she served in numerous convoys during World War II, transporting goods such as grain and coal. On 7 August 1944, while en route from Mozambique to Aden, she was torpedoed and sunk by German submarine U-198 off the coast of Tanganyika. All 42 aboard survived, though her Chief Officer was later killed when U-198 was sunk.

Ryan Higa (born June 6, 1990) is an American YouTuber, actor, and entrepreneur, best known for his comedic content on the YouTube channel *nigahiga*, which became one of the platform's most popular channels. Born in Hilo, Hawaii, Higa began creating videos in 2006 with friends while in high school, initially focusing on lip-syncing before expanding into comedy sketches and short films. His channel was the first to reach 3 million subscribers in 2010, and his content has amassed over 4.36 billion views. Higa also created a second channel, *HigaTV*, for vlogs and behind-the-scenes content.

Higa co-founded the parody K-pop group Boys Generally Asian (BgA), which achieved chart success with its comedic songs. He has also appeared in films, including *Ryan and Sean's Not So Excellent Adventure* (2008) and *Finding 'Ohana* (2021), and published a memoir, *Ryan Higa's How to Write Good* (2017). In addition to his entertainment career, Higa co-founded Ninja Melk, an energy drink company, in 2016. After stepping back from YouTube in 2020, he transitioned to Twitch, where he streams gaming content. Known for his creativity and humor, Higa remains a prominent figure in online entertainment.

The foreign trade of Pakistan plays a significant role in the country's economy, with exports and imports contributing to its economic activity. In the fiscal year 2015–2016, Pakistan's exports amounted to $21 billion, while imports stood at $44.76 billion. Over the years, Pakistan's exports have grown substantially, increasing from $7.5 billion in 1999 to $18 billion in 2007–2008, and reaching $29 billion by April 2015.

Pakistan's key exports include rice, textiles, clothing, leather goods, sports goods (notably footballs), surgical instruments, cement, seafood, processed food, and defense equipment such as submarines and tanks. The country also exports engineering goods, software, and agricultural products like mangoes and kinnows. Cement is a notable export to regions like Asia, the Middle East, and India. Russia has emerged as a growing market for Pakistani exports.

Pakistan maintains trade relations with various countries and has business ties with numerous international companies, reflecting its integration into the global trade network.

"Bizarre Love Triangle" is a song by the English rock band New Order, released as a single in 1986 from their fourth studio album, Brotherhood (1986), which reached the top five on the US Hot Dance Music/Club Play Singles chart, and No. 5 on the Australian ARIA Charts (No. 1 on the Victoria state chart) in March 1987.

It failed to make the top 40 in either the United Kingdom (only reaching No. 56) or the US Billboard Hot 100. In the United States, the song reached No. 8 on the Hot Dance Music/Maxi-Singles Sales chart, but failed to chart on the Hot 100 during its original 1986 release. However, a new mix included on The Best of New Order was released in 1994 and finally made a brief appearance on the Billboard Hot 100 in the No. 98 position in 1995.

In 2004, the song was ranked No. 204 in Rolling Stone's "The 500 Greatest Songs of All Time."

In algebraic topology, the Betti numbers are used to distinguish topological spaces based on the connectivity of n-dimensional simplicial complexes. For the most reasonable finite-dimensional spaces (such as compact manifolds, finite simplicial complexes or CW complexes), the sequence of Betti numbers is 0 from some point onward (Betti numbers vanish above the dimension of a space), and they are all finite.

The nth Betti number represents the rank of the nth homology group, denoted Hn, which tells us the maximum number of cuts that can be made before separating a surface into two pieces or 0-cycles, 1-cycles, etc. For example, if then, if then, if then, if then, etc. Note that only the ranks of infinite groups are considered, so for example if, where is the finite cyclic group of order 2, then. These finite components of the homology groups are their torsion subgroups, and they are denoted by torsion coefficients.

The term "Betti numbers" was coined by Henri Poincaré after Enrico Betti. The modern formulation is due to Emmy Noether. Betti numbers are used today in fields such as simplicial homology, computer science, digital images, etc.

*The Show Must Go On* is a 2007 South Korean crime drama film written and directed by Han Jae-rim. Starring Song Kang-ho, the film follows In-gu, a gangster struggling to balance his criminal career with his responsibilities as a husband and father. Released on April 5, 2007, it garnered over 1 million admissions and won Best Film and Best Actor at the 28th Blue Dragon Film Awards.

Courtney Andrew Walsh (born October 30, 1962) is a former Jamaican cricketer and one of the greatest fast bowlers in the history of cricket. Representing the West Indies from 1984 to 2001, Walsh played 132 Test matches and 205 One Day Internationals (ODIs), taking 519 and 227 wickets, respectively. He was the first bowler in Test cricket history to surpass 500 wickets and held the record for the most Test wickets until 2004. Known for his remarkable durability, accuracy, and sportsmanship, Walsh formed a legendary bowling partnership with Curtly Ambrose, sharing 421 wickets in 49 Tests.

Walsh also had a distinguished domestic career, playing for Jamaica and Gloucestershire County Cricket Club. He was named one of Wisden's Cricketers of the Year in 1987 and was inducted into the ICC Cricket Hall of Fame in 2010. Post-retirement, Walsh has served as a coach, including as head coach of the West Indies women's team. Renowned for his dedication and humility, Walsh remains a celebrated figure in cricket history.

Stephen James White (born 2 January 1959) is an English former footballer and manager, best known for his prolific goal-scoring career as a forward with clubs including Swindon Town, Hereford United, and Cardiff City.

Nicole Bilderback (born June 10, 1975) is a South Korean-born American actress known for her roles in film and television. Adopted by American parents and raised in Oklahoma and Texas, she began studying dance and acting at a young age, eventually enrolling at the Dallas Young Actor's Studio. After graduating from Lake Highlands High School in 1993, she moved to Los Angeles to pursue acting, debuting in the 1995 film *Clueless*. Bilderback has since appeared in various projects, including the *Clueless* television series and the 2014 action film *Mercenaries*.

Iris variegata, commonly known as the Hungarian iris, is a plant species in the genus Iris, also in the subgenus Iris. It is a rhizomatous perennial from eastern Europe. It has dark green, ribbed leaves. The branched flowering stems can be as tall as the leaves, they can hold 2–3 flowers in summer. They are yellowish-white, with brown-purple veins on the drooping falls. It is very hardy and it is commonly cultivated as an ornamental plant in temperate regions. There are several cultivars.

The 2018 New Hampshire elections were held on November 6, 2018, with primary elections on June 5. Democrats gained control of the New Hampshire Executive Council, State Senate, and State House of Representatives. Both U.S. House seats were retained by Democrats, while Republican Governor Chris Sununu was reelected. No U.S. Senate election was held in the state.

Cai Jinbiao (born 1954) is a Chinese former footballer who played as a defender. Known for his strong defensive skills and nicknamed "Steel Head," he played for Guangdong FC, helping them win their first league title in 1979, and represented the China national team from 1976 to 1982.

Charles Boarman (December 24, 1795 – September 13, 1879) was a career officer in the United States Navy. He entered the naval service shortly before the War of 1812 and served until 1876, subsequently retiring as a rear admiral. He held a number of important posts, both in peace and wartime, in the Mediterranean, West Indies and Brazil Squadrons and as commandant of the Brooklyn Navy Yard. He was also assigned to special duty during the American Civil War and a member of the U.S. Naval Board at Washington, DC.

After attending naval school at the Washington Navy Yard, Boarman saw service as a young midshipmen aboard USS Jefferson during the War of 1812 and later took part in anti-piracy operations in the early 1820s. He commanded a number of warships between 1827 and 1850, most notably, USS Brandywine during the Mexican–American War. In 1876, Boarman was promoted to rear admiral on the retired list and died in Martinsburg, West Virginia, three years later.

He was among several of Catholic background, such as John Cassin, Patrick McDonough, and Philemon C. Wederstrandt, to become high-ranking naval officers in the early years of the U.S. Navy. He was also, at the time of his death, the longest serving naval officer on the Navy Register with 68 years service. The Boarman family home, the Boarman House, occupied by the family for over a century, is a state historical landmark in West Virginia.

Matt Daniels (born September 27, 1989) is an American football coach and former player who serves as the special teams coordinator for the Minnesota Vikings of the National Football League (NFL). A standout safety at Duke University, he played in the NFL from 2012 to 2015 before transitioning to coaching.

Beta Delphini, also known as Rotanev, is a binary star system in the constellation Delphinus, located approximately 101 light-years from Earth. Discovered as a binary in 1873, it consists of two F-type stars with an orbital period of 26.66 years. The primary star is a giant with 1.75 times the Sun's mass, while the secondary is a subgiant with 1.47 times the Sun's mass. The system's name, Rotanev, originated as a reversed Latinized form of the name of Niccolò Cacciatore, an astronomer who worked on the Palermo Star Catalogue. In 2016, the International Astronomical Union officially approved the name Rotanev for the primary star.

*Dehraadun Diary* is a 2013 Hindi-language drama film directed by Milind Ukey and produced by Anita and Yatin Nandwani. Starring Adhyayan Suman, Ragini Nandwani, and Rati Agnihotri, the film explores themes of love, justice, and corruption. It follows the story of a young couple whose love is opposed by a powerful political family, leading to murder and a prolonged legal battle for justice. The film highlights the struggles of a grieving family and a conflicted young woman fighting against her influential family in a corrupt system.
Stevie Anderson (born May 12, 1970) is a former American football wide receiver who played in the National Football League.

John Patrick Rourke FMLS (born 26 March 1942, in Cape Town) is a South African botanist, who worked at the Kirstenbosch National Botanical Garden and became curator of the Compton Herbarium. He is a specialist in the flora of the Cape Floristic Region, in particular the family Proteaceae.

Donia Jessop (born 1970) is an American politician who serves as the current mayor of Hildale, Utah. Upon taking office, Jessop became the city’s first female mayor. For decades before Jessop's administration, Hildale was controlled by the Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints (FLDS Church). Jessop is Hildale's first mayor not endorsed by the FLDS Church.

Tauwharenīkau, also spelled Tauherenikau, is a locality in the South Wairarapa District of New Zealand's Wellington Region. Covering an area of 337.71 km², it had an estimated population of 1,500 as of June 2022, with a population density of 4.4 people per km². The area has experienced steady population growth, increasing by 39.2% between 2006 and 2018. The 2018 New Zealand census recorded a population of 1,353, with a median age of 47.5 years, higher than the national median. The community is predominantly European/Pākehā, with smaller proportions of Māori, Pacific peoples, and Asians. Tauwharenīkau has a higher proportion of residents with no religion compared to the national average, and its median income exceeds the national figure.
Sthenelaidas was a Spartan ephor, active during the late 5th century BCE, who played a pivotal role in Sparta's decision to go to war against Athens, initiating the Peloponnesian War.

Annette Beard (also known as Annette Helton, Annette Sterling, and Annette Simmons) is an American singer and a founding member of the Motown girl group Martha and the Vandellas. Born in Detroit, Michigan, she began her career in 1957 with The Del-Phis, which evolved into Martha and the Vandellas. Known for her contralto vocals, Beard contributed to hits like "(Love Is Like A) Heat Wave" and "Quicksand" before leaving the group in 1964 to focus on her family. She later reunited with the group for performances and was inducted into the Rock & Roll Hall of Fame in 1995.

*Jumping Flash! 2* is a first-person platform game developed by Exact and MuuMuu and published by Sony Computer Entertainment for the PlayStation. Released in 1996, it is the sequel to *Jumping Flash!* and retains much of its predecessor's gameplay, featuring a first-person perspective, triple-jump mechanics, and projectile-based combat. Players control Robbit, a robotic rabbit, as he rescues the inhabitants of Little Muu from the alien invader Captain Kabuki, who teams up with Robbit's former foe, Baron Aloha. The game introduces new features, including rescuing MuuMuus, a revamped interface, power-ups, and collectible performance medals.

The game was praised for its improved graphics, larger levels, and expanded features, though critics noted its short length and low difficulty. It was lauded for its innovative gameplay and visual design, with *Electronic Gaming Monthly* ranking it among the "100 Best Games of All Time" in 1997. The soundtrack, composed by Takeo Miratsu, was released in Japan alongside the game. *Jumping Flash! 2* was re-released on the PlayStation Network in 2009, maintaining its original gameplay and controls. Despite being considered more of an expansion than a groundbreaking sequel, it remains a notable entry in the early 3D platforming genre.

Län (Swedish, IPA-sv), lääni (Finnish, IPA-fi) and len (Norwegian, IPA-no) refer to the administrative divisions used in Sweden and previously in Finland and Norway. The provinces of Finland were abolished on January 1, 2010. In Norway, the term was in use between 1308 and 1662.

They are also sometimes used in other countries, especially as a translation of the Russian word volost. During the period when Finland was a part of the Russian Empire (1809-1917), when Russian was made an official language alongside Swedish, it was synonymous with the word guberniya.

Hugh Leslie OBE (17 April 1900 – 2 September 1974) was an Australian politician and newspaper editor. A member of the Australian Country Party, he served as the federal Member of Parliament for Moore from 1949 to 1958 and again from 1961 to 1963. Born in Durban, South Africa, Leslie emigrated to Australia in 1923 and became active in politics after a career in journalism. He also served in the military during World War II, losing his right leg in Libya. Leslie was later recognized with an Order of the British Empire (OBE) in 1967.

The Gryphon is a fictional character from Lewis Carroll's *Alice's Adventures in Wonderland*, illustrated by John Tenniel. A resident of Wonderland, he is overbearing and dismissive, often mocking others' concerns. He appears alongside the Mock Turtle in several chapters, guiding Alice and explaining Wonderland's nonsensical features.

The Materfer CMM 400-2 is an Argentine diesel multiple unit (DMU) manufactured by Materfer in Córdoba Province. Designed for various track gauges, it features stainless steel construction, Scania engines, and modern passenger amenities. Operated by Trenes Argentinos, it serves urban and inter-urban routes across Argentina, including the Mitre, Roca, Urquiza, and Belgrano railways.

E. S. Rose Park is a 25-acre multi-use sports facility located in the Edgehill neighborhood of Nashville, Tennessee. Owned by the Nashville Metro Parks & Recreation Board, it serves as the home for Belmont University's baseball, softball, soccer, and track and field teams, as well as local sports leagues. Renovated between 2010 and 2011 at a cost of $8 million, the park opened in April 2011 and features both artificial turf and natural grass surfaces.

In Hindustani music (North Indian classical music), a gharānā is a system of social organisation in the Indian subcontinent, linking musicians or dancers by lineage or apprenticeship, and more importantly by adherence to a particular musical style.

The word gharana comes from the Hindi word 'ghar' which is derived from the Sanskrit word Griha, which means 'house'. It typically refers to the place where the musical ideology originated; for example, some of the gharanas well known for singing khyals are: Dilli(Delhi), Agra, Gwalior, Indore, Atrauli-Jaipur, Kirana and Patiala. Four famous kathak gharanas are: Lucknow, Atrauli-Jaipur, Benares and Raigarh.

St. James' Cemetery is a historic cemetery in Toronto, Ontario, Canada. It is the oldest cemetery in Toronto that is still in use, having opened in 1844. It was originally the burial ground for the Cathedral Church of St. James, but it later became non-denominational. The main entrance to the cemetery is located at 635 Parliament Street, north of Wellesley Street East. Just to the west is the St. James Town neighbourhood, which is named after the cemetery.

The Utría National Natural Park (Lang-es) is a national park in the Chocó Department, Colombia. It contains diverse flora and fauna in a lush, mountainous rainforest environment with some of the highest rainfall in the world, at up to 10,000 millimetres (390 in) annually. The park also protects the coastal marine environment, and is known for visits by humpback whales, who give birth in the lagoon after which the park is named, and sea turtles who nest on the beaches. There is accommodation for visitors, and ecotourism services are provided by the local indigenous people and members of the coastal Afro-Colombian communities.

Beatrice Wishart (born 1955 or 1956) is a Scottish Liberal Democrats politician who has been the Member of the Scottish Parliament (MSP) for Shetland since 2019. Wishart currently serves as education spokesperson for her party, and has a seat on the Scottish Parliament committees for Education and Skills, for Culture, Tourism, Europe and External Affairs, and for COVID-19. She was elected at the 2019 Shetland by-election, after the sitting Liberal Democrat MSP Tavish Scott stepped down.

Pelican Waters is a suburb of Caloundra in the Sunshine Coast Region, Queensland, Australia. In the 2016 census, Pelican Waters had a population of 6,277 people.

Progress MS-20 (Lang-ru), Russian production No.450, identified by NASA as Progress 81P, is a Progress spaceflight launched by Roscosmos to resupply the International Space Station (ISS). It is the 173rd flight of a Progress spacecraft.

Siniperca chuatsi, the mandarin fish (Chinese: 鱖魚,), is a species of freshwater ray-finned fish from the family Sinipercidae, the Oriental perches. It is the type species of the genus Siniperca (Chinese perches).

The November 1992 Venezuelan coup attempt was a failed military uprising against the government of President Carlos Andrés Pérez. Led by dissident air force and navy officers, the coup sought to address widespread discontent over economic instability and corruption. Despite initial successes, the rebellion was suppressed, resulting in 172 deaths and 95 injuries.
Kleiner Tiergarten ("Small Tiergarten") is a park in Moabit, Berlin, Germany.
Freerunner is a 2011 action film by Lawrence Silverstein starring Sean Faris, Danny Dyer and Tamer Hassan in lead roles.

George Washington's reception at Trenton took place on April 21, 1789, during his journey to New York City for his inauguration as the first president of the United States. The event featured a grand celebration, including a triumphal arch commemorating his Revolutionary War victories at Trenton, adorned with laurel and flowers, and a banner reading, "The Defender of the Mothers Will Also Protect Their Daughters." Local women and children, dressed in white, sang a sonata and scattered flowers in his path. The reception has been commemorated through re-enactments, historical markers, and numerous artistic depictions.

Lygodium microphyllum (commonly known as, variously, climbing maidenhair fern, Old World climbing fern, small-leaf climbing fern, or snake fern) is a climbing fern originating in tropical Africa, Southeast Asia, Melanesia and Australia. It is an invasive weed in Florida where it invades open forest and wetland areas. The type specimen was collected in the vicinity of Nabúa, on the island of Luzon in the Philippines by Luis Née.

Gavan McCormack is a researcher specializing in East Asia who is Emeritus Professor and Visiting Fellow, Division of Pacific and Asian History of the Australian National University. He is also a coordinator of an award-winning open access journal The Asia-Pacific Journal: Japan Focus.
Stephen Soter Ortynsky Hentosh, O.S.B.M. (Lang-uk; 29 January 1866 – 24 March 1916) was the first Bishop of all Greek Catholics in the United States.

The Maine Italian sandwich is a traditional sandwich originating in Portland, Maine, consisting of a long bread roll filled with ham, cheese, tomato, onion, green bell pepper, olives, and oil. It was invented by Giovanni Amato, founder of Amato's sandwich shops.

Artillerie-Inrichtingen (A.I.) was a Dutch state-owned arms and munitions manufacturer founded in 1679 in Delft by Willem III of Orange. It played a significant role in producing artillery, small arms, and precision machinery, including the AR-10 battle rifle, which influenced modern firearm designs. Relocated to Zaandam in 1899, the company expanded its operations and contributed to Dutch military efforts. After World War II, it diversified into civilian products. A.I. was split in 1973 into Eurometaal and Hembrug Machine Tools, with the latter continuing to produce precision lathes.

Whitland Abbey, located in Carmarthenshire, Wales, was a Cistercian monastery founded on September 16, 1140, by monks from Clairvaux Abbey. Initially established at Little Trefgarn near Haverfordwest, it moved to Whitland around 1155. The abbey became a significant cultural and religious center in southwest Wales, known for its support of Welsh independence and its association with Rhys ap Gruffudd, Prince of Deheubarth. It founded several daughter houses, including Strata Florida Abbey and Strata Marcella Abbey. The abbey flourished until its dissolution in the 16th century, after which the site was repurposed, eventually becoming part of the Yelverton family estate.
Since the early 2020s, claims have been made on social media that there is a deliberate campaign by governments to create an artificial famine.
Soter Ortynsky (1866–1916) was the first bishop of the Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church in the United States, known for establishing orphanages, a cathedral, and fostering church independence.
Roderick Bradley (born March 29, 1983) is a British athlete, American football player, Gladiators TV personality, and sports entrepreneur.

*Terrace House: Closing Door* (テラスハウス クロージング・ドア) is a 2015 Japanese film directed by Masato Maeda, serving as a continuation and conclusion to the reality television series *Terrace House: Boys × Girls Next Door*. The film follows the lives and relationships of new and returning house members as they navigate romance, personal growth, and career aspirations within the shared living space. Released on February 14, 2015, the film was produced by Fuji Television and East Entertainment and distributed by Toho. It debuted at number one at the Japanese box office, grossing ¥1.3 billion in total. The film's soundtrack, released by Universal Music, reached number 34 on the Oricon Albums Chart. *Terrace House: Closing Door* marked the end of the original *Terrace House* series.

StarUML is a proprietary Unified Modeling Language (UML) tool developed by MKLabs, initially released in 2006. It evolved from the earlier Plastic software and supports UML 2.0, SysML, and legacy modeling notations. Originally open-source, StarUML transitioned to a commercial model in 2014, with the latest version, 5.0, released in 2022. It is compatible with Windows, macOS, and Linux, offering features like diagram generation, model documentation, and file format support for JSON, HTML, and image exports. StarUML is widely used in academia and software modeling, ranking as the third most-used tool in a 2017 study.

Matthew Asen Kantakouzenos (c. 1325 – 1383 or 1391) was a Byzantine emperor who reigned from 1353 to 1357 during a period of civil war. The son of Emperor John VI Kantakouzenos, he was granted Thrace as an appanage and later proclaimed co-emperor. His reign was marked by conflicts with John V Palaiologos and military campaigns against the Serbs, culminating in his capture and forced abdication. Afterward, he governed the Morea from 1380 to 1383 before ceding power to his son, Demetrios I Kantakouzenos.
The Light Helicopter Experimental (LHX) program was a 1980s United States Army helicopter procurement project to replace the AH-1 Cobra and OH-58 Kiowa helicopters.

A chase gun (or chaser), usually distinguished as bow chaser and stern chaser, was a cannon mounted in the bow (aiming forward) or stern (aiming backward) of a sailing ship. They were used to attempt to slow down an enemy ship either chasing (pursuing) or being chased, when the ship's broadside could not be brought to bear. Typically, the chasers were used to attempt to damage the rigging and thereby cause the target to lose performance.

Bow chasers could be regular guns brought up from the gundeck and aimed through specially cut-out ports on either side of the bowsprit, or dedicated weapons made with an unusually long bore and a relatively light ball, and mounted in the bow. Stern chasers could also be improvised, or left permanently in the cabins at the stern, covered up and used as part of the furniture.

Alice – A Fight For Life is a documentary featuring 47-year-old Alice Jefferson, a British woman who developed malignant pleural mesothelioma thirty years after working for nine months at Cape Insulation's Acre Mill asbestos plant in Hebden Bridge, West Yorkshire. The film also explored the health issues surrounding the manufacture and use of asbestos products. Described by The Guardian newspaper as "a momentous film", the programme also explicitly linked asbestos with cancer, and attacked what it perceived as the government's complacency in limiting the manufacture and use of asbestos in Britain.

Alice died of mesothelioma in February 1982, a month after filming for the programme had ended. She left two children: Paul and Patsy aged, 15 and 5 respectively.

John Thomas Gordon Ruddy (born 24 October 1986) is an English professional footballer who plays as a goalkeeper for EFL League One club Birmingham City.

Ruddy began his career at Cambridge United, before signing for Everton as a 19-year-old. He spent five years with Everton, but made just one appearance, while playing on loan at nine different clubs. Ruddy was signed by Paul Lambert to play for Norwich City in 2010 and, in his first season, was part of the team that finished second in the Championship, gaining promotion to the Premier League.

The following year, Norwich finished twelfth in the Premier League, and Ruddy was selected by new England manager Roy Hodgson for the squad for Euro 2012. However, on 25 May 2012, he was ruled out after breaking his finger during training. He made his debut in a 2–1 win against Italy in August 2012. He suffered a long term thigh injury in November 2012, but returned to help Norwich to an 11th-place finish at the end of the 2012–13 Premier League season. After 243 appearances for Norwich, he moved to Wolverhampton Wanderers in 2017. After one season as first choice as Wolves gained promotion to the Premier League, he spent four years as backup, and left the club at the end of the 2021–22 season. He then joined Birmingham City.

Pivotal (19 January 1993 – 19 November 2021) was a British Thoroughbred racehorse and sire. In a racing career restricted to six races between October 1995 and August 1996 he established himself as one of the leading sprinters in Europe. His most important wins came in the King's Stand Stakes and the Nunthorpe Stakes as a three-year-old in the summer of 1996. He was then retired to stud where he became an exceptionally successful breeding stallion.

Jeff Danielson (born August 6, 1970) is an American politician and former firefighter who served as a Democratic member of the Iowa Senate from 2005 to 2019, representing District 10 and later District 30 following redistricting. During his tenure, he served as President Pro Tempore of the Iowa Senate from 2006 to 2012 and was a strong advocate for veterans' issues as Vice-Chair of the Veterans Affairs Committee. Danielson also explored legislation on online poker regulation in Iowa. 

A native of Waterloo, Iowa, he holds degrees from Hawkeye Community College and the University of Northern Iowa. In early 2019, Danielson resigned from the Senate, citing family and financial considerations, and later became a lobbyist for the American Wind Energy Association. A special election was held to fill his seat.

The Light Helicopter Experimental (LHX) program was a U.S. Army initiative launched in 1982 to develop a next-generation helicopter, ultimately resulting in the RAH-66 Comanche. Initially intended to replace multiple helicopter models, the program faced challenges including shifting requirements, technological risks, and rising costs. After selecting the Boeing-Sikorsky design in 1991, the program was terminated in 2004 due to budget constraints, evolving mission priorities, and unachievable requirements.

Terrace House: Closing Door (Japanese: テラスハウス クロージング・ドア, Hepburn: Terasu Hausu Kurōjingu Doa) is a 2015 Japanese reality show theatrical film that acts as a continuation and ending to the Fuji Television show Terrace House: Boys × Girls Next Door. It was released by Toho on February 14, 2015. Tetsuya Sugaya and Seina Shimabukuro continue as residents from the show, with four new members joining them. One of whom is writing a book on Terrace House and briefly interviews previous members. Studio commentators You, Reina Triendl, Yoshimi Tokui, Azusa Babazono, Ryota Yamasato and Hiroomi Tosaka also return.

Closing Door is the last installment in the Terrace House franchise before Netflix became a co-producer later in the year with Terrace House: Boys & Girls in the City.
*Madonna and Child Kissing* is an oil-on-panel painting by Quentin Matsys, created circa 1525–1530. Depicting Mary and Jesus in a richly decorated setting, it is housed at the Mauritshuis, The Hague.

Titus Lucretius Carus (c. 99 BC – c. 55 BC) was a Roman poet and philosopher, best known for his didactic epic *De rerum natura* (*On the Nature of Things*), which introduced Epicurean philosophy to Roman audiences. Written in six books of poetic hexameters, the work explores atomism, the nature of the mind and soul, the development of the universe, and the evolution of human society, rejecting divine intervention in favor of naturalistic explanations. Lucretius also theorized about the progression of human technology, proposing a sequence of tools and materials—stone, copper, bronze, and iron—that prefigured the modern three-age system.

Little is known about Lucretius's life. Ancient sources suggest he was born in the 90s BC and died in the 50s BC, possibly by suicide, though such claims are often dismissed as speculative or biased. His work was praised by contemporaries like Cicero and Virgil for its intellectual depth and poetic brilliance.

Lucretius's natural philosophy anticipated elements of evolutionary theory, emphasizing nature's experimentation and the survival of well-adapted organisms. However, he rejected the idea of species evolving from one another and denied the spherical Earth theory. Despite his influence waning in antiquity, *De rerum natura* was rediscovered in the Renaissance, profoundly shaping modern scientific and philosophical thought.

Joseph Conrad is an iron-hulled sailing ship, originally launched as Georg Stage in 1882 and used to train sailors in Denmark. After sailing around the world as a private yacht in 1934 she served as a training ship in the United States, and is now a museum ship at Mystic Seaport in Connecticut.

A marine evacuation system (MES) is a life-saving appliance designed for the rapid and efficient evacuation of passengers and crew from ships during emergencies. Over the past 30 years, it has gained popularity for its ability to evacuate over 700 people within 30 minutes, making it a critical safety feature in the maritime industry. The system has been rigorously tested under challenging conditions, including Beaufort Sea State 6 winds and 3-meter wave heights, and complies with the EU Directive of Marine Equipment 96/98/EC and IMO Resolution A689(17).

The MES is designed for quick deployment, requiring only one or two crew members to activate. Once deployed, a chute extends into the water, and an inflatable life raft is automatically activated within 90 seconds, with some systems inflating in under a minute. Passengers descend through air-cushioned chutes into the rafts, which are capable of accommodating over 700 individuals. The system consists of five main components.

Yamada Amy (山田 詠美, Yamada Eimi) born February 8, 1959, is a popular but controversial contemporary Japanese writer who is most famous for her stories that address issues of sexuality, racism, and interracial love and marriage. Her debut and subsequent popular success in the 1990s was a part of Japan's hip-hop and Black culture boom. While she is most known for her stories of complicated and messy romantic love, she also writes on the daily minutiae of life (slice-of-life), child-raising, and bullying.

Baaji (‹See RfD› transl. elder sister) is a 2019 Pakistani romantic drama film, directed by Saqib Malik and produced by his Production company Page 33 Films. The film features Meera as Shameera Osman Khalid Butt as Rohail Khan/ Khalid Osman, Amna Ilyas as Neha, Mohsin Abbas Haider as Ajji, Ali Kazmi as Rammy, Nayyar Ejaz, and Nisho in pivotal roles. It is a dramatic tale of a fading Lollywood actress Shameera (Meera), who is not accepting that now her position in the industry is not much valuable. The story revolves around the relationship of Shameera with a Salon girl Neha (Amna Ilyas) who later becomes an assistant of Shameera and make this opportunity happens of working with an American Based Pakistani Director Rohail Khan (Osman Khalid Butt). Neha who belongs to a conservative lower middle class family then also make herself involved in Shameera's comeback movie titled Waapsi by helping them in rehearsals and script making so far she get a chance to take a supporting role in the movie. The film includes Ali Kazmi as Raamy (Shameera's Boyfriend) who is highly aggressive about Shameera. Mohsin Abbas Haider plays the love interest of neha's character, while the Legendary actor Nayyer Ejaz supports the film by his great comics and unforgettable role as Chand Kamal, film also includes numerous well-known faces from the industry as in cameo appearances.

RPT Realty, formerly known as RAMCO Properties Trust and Ramco-Gershenson Properties Trust, is a publicly traded real estate investment trust that owns and operates open-air shopping centers. As of August 4, 2021, the company owned 50 shopping centers comprising 12.6 million square feet.

Notable properties owned by the company include The Shops on Lane Avenue in Upper Arlington, Ohio; Woodbury Lakes in Woodbury, Minnesota; Bedford Marketplace in Bedford, Massachusetts; Lakehills Plaza in Austin, Texas; River City Marketplace in Jacksonville, Florida; Mission Bay Plaza in Boca Raton, Florida; The Crossroads in Royal Palm Beach, Florida; Coral Creek Shops in Coconut Creek, Florida.
The alternative Christmas message is a message broadcast by Channel 4 since 1993, as a sometimes humorous and sometimes serious alternative to the traditional Royal Christmas Message.
Kylie Nicole Sicangco Padilla (IPA-tl; born 25 January 1993) is a Filipino actress, singer and model. She is the daughter of actor Robin Padilla and Liezl Sicangco.

Hallie Flanagan (August 27, 1889 – June 23, 1969) was an American theatrical producer, director, playwright, and author, best known for directing the Federal Theatre Project during the Great Depression. A pioneer of experimental theater, she was the first woman awarded a Guggenheim Fellowship to study theater in Europe. Flanagan also founded the Vassar Experimental Theatre and later served as Dean and professor at Smith College. Her leadership of the Federal Theatre Project brought innovative, affordable theater to millions, though it faced political controversy and was ultimately shut down.

Valmiera (pronunciation; Lang-de; Lang-pl see other names) is the largest city of the historical Vidzeme region, Latvia, with a total area of 19.35 square kilometres (7 square miles). As of 2002, Valmiera had a population of 27,323, and in 2020 – 24 879. It is a state city the seat of Valmiera Municipality.

Valmiera lies at the crossroads of several important roads, 107 kilometres (66 miles) to the north-east from Riga, the capital of Latvia, and 50 km (31 mi) south of the border with Estonia. Valmiera lies on both banks of the Gauja River.
Madonna and Child Kissing is a 1520s oil on panel painting by the Flemish renaissance artist Quentin Matsys in the collection of the Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam, on loan to the Mauritshuis.

The 1912–13 season was Blackpool F.C.'s 16th season in the Football League. Competing in the Second Division, they finished 20th and last, resulting in relegation. George Wilson was the team's top scorer with ten goals. In the FA Cup, they exited in the first round.

Muthoot Microfin Limited is a non-banking financial company (NBFC-MFI) headquartered in Mumbai, India. Established in 2012, it is part of the Muthoot Pappachan Group and focuses on microfinance services.

The Most Eminent Order of the Indian Empire is an order of chivalry founded by Queen Victoria on 1 January 1878. The Order includes members of three classes:

No appointments have been made since 1947, the year that British India gained independence as the Union of India and Dominion of Pakistan. With the death of the last surviving knight, the Maharaja Meghrajji III of Dhrangadhra, the order became dormant in 2010.

The motto of the Order is Imperatricis auspiciis, (Latin for "Under the auspices of the Empress"), a reference to Queen Victoria, the first Empress of India. The Order is the junior British order of chivalry associated with the British Indian Empire; the senior one is The Most Exalted Order of the Star of India.

Barley yellow mosaic virus is plant pathogenic virus that causes the yellow mosaic disease of barley. Its shape is categorized as being flexuous filamentous, with lengths of 275 and 550 nanometers. The virus has a limited host range, and barley appears to be the only known susceptible host. The virus is transmitted via Polymyxa graminis, which is a plasmodiophorid protist, through the resting spores that survive in the soil, and eventually zoospores. Eastern Asia is the most affected region, but the virus can be found worldwide. Current agricultural practices have been ineffective at eliminating the virus, but breeding resistance appears to be the only way to help reduce the disease.
*Puschkinia peshmenii* is a flowering plant species in the family Asparagaceae, native to southeastern Turkey and western Iran.

Purcari is a historic winery located in the Ștefan Vodă district of Moldova, renowned for its premium wines and rich heritage. Established in 1827 by decree of Emperor Nicholas I of Russia, it became the first specialized winery in Bessarabia. Purcari gained international acclaim in 1878 when its Negru de Purcari wine won a gold medal at the Paris World Expo, impressing French experts who mistook it for Bordeaux. The winery's wines were once served to European royalty, including Emperor Nicholas II and Queen Victoria.

After a period of decline during the Soviet era, Purcari was revitalized in 2003, restoring its historic cellars and production techniques. Today, it produces a wide range of wines, including its iconic vintage collections, and exports to over 25 countries.

Hatton Castle is a 16th-century castle located near Newtyle in Angus, Scotland. Built in 1575 by Laurence, 4th Lord Oliphant, it features a rare scale and platt staircase and served as the residence of the Masters of Oliphant. The castle was de-roofed in the early 18th century after the Jacobite rising but was faithfully restored in the 20th century, incorporating traditional materials and modern amenities. Now a family home, it hosts musical events and gatherings, including performances by Scottish traditional musicians. The surrounding gardens feature dry-stone walls, an orchard, and remnants of the historic Dundee and Newtyle Railway.

*Crossing the Red Sea with The Adverts* is the 1978 debut studio album by English punk rock band The Adverts. Recorded at Abbey Road Studios, it received critical acclaim for its raw energy and is considered a landmark of the punk era. The album, featuring the single "No Time to Be 21," reached No. 38 on the UK Albums Chart and has been praised as one of the finest albums of the 1970s. It was later ranked No. 17 in *Mojo* magazine's list of the 50 greatest punk albums and included in *The Guardian*'s "1000 Albums to Hear Before You Die."

*Chaos of the Senses* is a 1997 Arabic novel by Ahlam Mosteghanemi. Set during the Algerian Civil War, it follows Hayat, a novelist in a loveless marriage, as her writing blurs with reality, exploring themes of love, identity, and Algeria's struggles.
Kate Elizabeth Russell (born 1984) is an American author. Her debut novel, My Dark Vanessa, was published in 2020 and became a national bestseller.

The Holliday Transfer Facility (TDCJ Identification Code: NF, also referred to as the Holliday Transfer Unit), is a Texas Department of Criminal Justice transfer facility for men located in Huntsville, Texas. Holliday is along Interstate 45 and.5 miles (0.80 km) north of Texas State Highway 30. The unit, on a 1,412-acre (571 ha) plot of land, is co-located with the Wynne Unit. 

Holliday, one of the largest transfer facilities in Texas, is across the street from the Texas Prison Museum. Holliday is one of two prisons in the TDCJ that, as of 2003, is named after an African-American.
Joseph Henry Rodriguez (born December 12, 1930) is a Senior United States district judge of the United States District Court for the District of New Jersey.

Andrée Geulen-Herscovici (6 September 1921 – 31 May 2022) was a Belgian teacher and member of the resistance during the German occupation of Belgium during World War II. After becoming aware of the persecution of Belgian Jews through her employment, she became involved in the Committee for the Defence of Jews (Comité de Défense des Juifs, or CDJ; Joods Verdedigingscomité, JVC) in 1943 and actively assisted with the organisation and protection of "hidden children" amid the Holocaust in Belgium. In the aftermath of the war, she was recognised as Righteous among the Nations by the Israeli institute Yad Vashem and received several other honours in Belgium and Israel.

The 2000 Save Mart/Kragen 350 was the 16th stock car race of the 2000 NASCAR Winston Cup Series and the 12th iteration of the event. The race was held on Sunday, June 25, 2000, in Sonoma, California, at the club layout in Sears Point Raceway, a 1.99 miles (3.20 km) permanent road course layout. The race took the scheduled 112 laps to complete. At race's end, Hendrick Motorsports driver Jeff Gordon would dominate the late stages of a chaotic race to win his 51st career NASCAR Winston Cup Series win and his first of the season. To fill out the podium, Sterling Marlin of Team SABCO and Mark Martin of Roush Racing would finish second and third, respectively.

The Allied Commission was a series of international bodies established by the Allies during and after World War II to oversee the administration and compliance of Axis powers and occupied territories with armistice agreements and peace treaties. These commissions operated in countries such as Italy, Romania, Finland, Bulgaria, Hungary, Germany, Austria, and Japan, with varying structures and leadership. Their roles included supervising disarmament, political reforms, and war crime trials. Most commissions were dissolved following the conclusion of peace treaties or the restoration of sovereignty.
Léonie Thévenot d'Aunet (2 July 1820 – 3 March 1879) was a French author, novelist, playwright and Arctic explorer.

*Corbett and Courtney Before the Kinetograph* is an 1894 American silent film produced by the Edison Manufacturing Company and directed by William K.L. Dickson. It features a staged six-round boxing match between heavyweight champion James J. Corbett and underdog Peter Courtney, filmed at the Black Maria studio in West Orange, New Jersey, the first movie studio in the United States. The film was created for viewing on a Kinetoscope and is one of the earliest sports films. Now in the public domain, it is preserved by the Library of Congress.

Yvette Paula Fielding (born 23 September 1968) is an English television presenter, producer and actress. She became the youngest presenter on Blue Peter aged 18, and one of her episodes was subsequently voted the "Favourite Blue Peter moment" ever. With her husband Karl Beattie, she presented the Most Haunted series on the Living channel, via their own production company, followed by Ghosthunting With., establishing Fielding as 'first lady of the paranormal'. She has appeared in a wide range of other programmes, from The Wright Stuff to Through the Keyhole and I'm A Celebrity. Get Me Out of Here!.

Melchior, traditionally known as one of the Three Magi or Wise Men, is venerated as a saint in various Christian traditions, including the Roman Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, Anglican, and Lutheran Churches. Believed to be the King of Persia, he is said to have followed the Star of Bethlehem to worship the Child Jesus, offering gold as a gift symbolizing Jesus' kingship. According to tradition, Melchior died on January 1, 55 AD, at the age of 116. His relics, along with those of the other Magi, are enshrined in the Shrine of the Three Kings at Cologne Cathedral. Melchior is commemorated on the Feast of Epiphany and is associated with various patronages, including travelers and pilgrims.
Russell Springs is a city in Logan County, Kansas, United States. As of the 2020 census, the population of the city was 26.
This article lists the squads of the women's hockey competition at the 2010 Commonwealth Games held in New Delhi, India from 4 to 13 October 2010.

City Tower is a modernist high-rise building in Manchester, England, completed in 1965 as part of the Piccadilly Plaza complex. Standing 123 meters tall with 30 floors, it serves as an office, retail, and communication hub, hosting major radio transmitters. Owned by Schroders since 2014, it features refurbished commercial spaces and a distinctive design.

Trelawne (Lang-kw, meaning elm-tree homestead) is an historic manor in the parish of Pelynt in Cornwall, England, situated 20 miles (32 km) west of Plymouth, Devon and four miles (6.4 km) west-northwest of Looe, Cornwall. It was long the seat of the Trelawny family, later Trelawny baronets, one of the most eminent of Cornish gentry families, much of whose political power derived from their control of the pocket borough of nearby East Looe. The surviving grade II* listed manor house known as Trelawne House is today used for holiday accommodation and entertainment, under the name "Trelawne Manor Holiday Park".

Donia Jessop (born 1970) is an American politician and activist serving as the mayor of Hildale, Utah, since January 4, 2018. She is the first female mayor of Hildale and the first not endorsed by the Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints (FLDS Church). A former FLDS member, Jessop left the church in 2012 and later co-founded the Short Creek Community Alliance to advocate for non-church members. As mayor, she has focused on modernization, infrastructure, economic development, and tourism. In 2020, she was named one of Utah's most influential women by *USA Today*.

Hoang Anh Gia Lai Group (HAGL) is a Vietnamese joint-stock company founded in 1990 by Đoàn Nguyên Đức and headquartered in Pleiku, Gia Lai. Initially a furniture manufacturer, HAGL has diversified into industries such as real estate, rubber, sugar, energy, mining, and football. It operates rubber plantations across Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia, and has expanded into hydroelectric and sugar production. The company is also active in real estate, including major projects in Vietnam, Laos, and Myanmar. HAGL has faced controversy over land disputes in Cambodia and Laos, with allegations of environmental and social violations involving indigenous communities.

Eddy Naït Slimani (born August 25, 1993) is a French professional kickboxer competing in the super bantamweight division. Known for his orthodox stance, he has achieved multiple tournament victories and held several titles, including the WKN European and NDC -65 kg championships. Slimani has fought in prominent promotions such as Glory and Superkombat Fighting Championship, facing notable opponents like Saenchai and Masaaki Noiri. Over his career, he has amassed a professional record of 45 fights, with 38 wins, including 8 by knockout.

"Heartache Tonight" is a song written by Don Henley, Glenn Frey, Bob Seger and J. D. Souther, recorded by the Eagles and features Glenn Frey on lead vocals. The track was included on their album The Long Run and released as a single in 1979. It reached No. 1 on the U.S. Billboard Hot 100 in November of that year and was certified Platinum by the Recording Industry Association of America representing one million copies sold. It was the Eagles' final chart-topping song on the Hot 100.

The 1945 Katsuyama killing incident was the murder of three African-American United States Marines in Katsuyama near Nago, Okinawa after the Battle of Okinawa on July 10, 1945 to August 13, 1946. Residents of Katsuyama had reportedly killed the three Marines for their repeated rape of village women during occupation of Okinawa and hid their bodies in a nearby cave out of fear for retaliation. The Katsuyama incident was kept secret until August 16, 1997 when the bodies and identities of the Marines were discovered.

Academic ranks in the United States encompass a hierarchy of faculty positions, including tenure-track roles like assistant, associate, and full professor, as well as non-tenure-track positions such as lecturers, instructors, and clinical professors. Additional titles include adjunct faculty, research personnel, and administrative roles like deans and department chairs. The system varies by institution and field, with distinctions in responsibilities, tenure eligibility, and promotion criteria.

Fishguard and Goodwick is the name of a community that wraps around Fishguard Bay, on the northern coast of Pembrokeshire, Wales. It comprises the two towns of Fishguard and Goodwick, with their neighbourhoods of Dyffryn, Harbour Village, Penyraber, Lower Town, and Stop-and-Call. Within the community are two railway stations and Goodwick Ferry Terminal, which is the terminus of the A40 London to Fishguard Trunk Road.

The 1952 Mysore State Legislative Assembly election was held on 26 March 1952 to elect 99 members to the Mysore Legislative Assembly. The Indian National Congress (INC) emerged victorious, and K. Hanumanthaiah became the Chief Minister, succeeding K. Chengalaraya Reddy.

*The Meaning of Life* is an Irish television interview series hosted by Gay Byrne, which aired on RTÉ One from April 19, 2009, to October 16, 2016. Spanning 12 series and 50 episodes, the show featured in-depth interviews with notable figures, exploring their views on life, faith, and personal beliefs. It received mixed reviews, with some critics noting its religious undertones and thought-provoking discussions.

Lincoln is a census-designated place in Lewis and Clark County, Montana, known for its historical significance, outdoor recreation, and proximity to wilderness areas. Established during the gold rush of the mid-19th century, it later became a commercial center for logging and mining. Lincoln gained national attention in 1996 with the arrest of Ted Kaczynski, the "Unabomber," near the town. It was also the site of a 5.8-magnitude earthquake in 2017. Located along Montana Highway 200, Lincoln features a humid continental climate and is near Rogers Pass, where the coldest temperature in the contiguous United States was recorded. The town is served by Lincoln Public School District and has a public library.

Bernard Vince (born 2 October 1985) is a former Australian rules footballer who played for the Adelaide and Melbourne Football Club in the Australian Football League (AFL). He has since become involved in the football media, working for Triple M and Fox Footy.

At the time of his retirement, Vince was only one of seven players in VFL/AFL history to have played 100 or more games at two clubs and win the best and fairest award at both clubs.

Gemeinhardt Co. is a manufacturer of flutes and piccolos. These musical instruments are developed by this company for all levels of musicians, beginners to professionals. It is owned by its major supplier, Angel Industries Co. Ltd of Taiwan, widely acknowledged as the premier manufacturer of woodwind musical instruments. The Gemeinhardt Company is headquartered in Elkhart, Indiana, where many of its instruments are still made.

Our Lady and St Peter's Church is a Roman Catholic parish church in Bridlington, East Riding of Yorkshire, England. It was built from 1893 to 1894 in the Gothic Revival style. It is located on the corner of Victoria Road and Wycliffe Lane, close to the town centre. It is a Grade II listed building.

Cai Jinbiao (Chinese: 蔡锦标; born 1954) is a former Chinese international footballer who played as a defender. He represented Guangdong FC where he won the 1979 Chinese league title as well as playing for the Chinese national football team in the 1980 Asian Cup.

Heber Springs is a city in Cleburne County, Arkansas, United States, with a population of 6,969 as of the 2020 census. Founded in 1882 as Sugar Loaf, it was renamed in 1910 to honor Dr. Heber Jones. The city is known for its natural springs, Greers Ferry Lake, and the Little Red River, which attract tourism for fishing, boating, and outdoor activities. Heber Springs serves as the county seat and features historical landmarks like the Clarence Frauenthal House and Greers Ferry Dam, which was dedicated by President John F. Kennedy in 1963.

Bilston is a village in Midlothian, Scotland, with a population of 1,440 as of 2022. Historically tied to the coal-mining industry, it is located near Bilston Glen and its woodland, a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI). The village is notable for its proximity to Dryden Tower, Bilston Burn, and its role in environmental protests against a proposed bypass through the SSSI. Bilston also features a primary school and a Police Scotland control room.
Veniamin Lvovich Beylin (1904–1982) was a Soviet major general, World War II commander, and military instructor.

The *German tanker Spichern* was originally launched in 1935 as *Krossfonn*, a Norwegian oil tanker built by Odense Staalskibsværft in Denmark. During World War II, it was captured by Germany in 1940, renamed *Spichern*, and converted into a naval oiler. Damaged in an Allied air raid in 1944, it was scuttled as a blockship. Post-war, the wreck was salvaged, rebuilt, and returned to Norwegian service under various names, including *Ringfjell* and *Ringsaker*, before being scrapped in 1964.
Ciudad del Plata is a city in San José Department of Uruguay.
Ciudad del Plata is a city in San José Department, Uruguay, established in 2006.
The 2022–23 season is the 104th in the history of AS Saint-Étienne and their first season back in the second division since 2004. The club are participating in Ligue 2 and the Coupe de France.

The Cello Concerto is a composition for cello and orchestra by the Finnish composer Esa-Pekka Salonen. The work was co-commissioned by the Chicago Symphony Orchestra, the New York Philharmonic, the Barbican Centre, and the Elbphilharmonie. It was completed in 2017 and was first performed by the cellist Yo-Yo Ma and the Chicago Symphony Orchestra under the direction of Salonen on March 9, 2017. The piece is dedicated to Yo-Yo Ma.

In the mathematical discipline of general topology, Stone–Čech compactification (or Čech–Stone compactification) is a technique for constructing a universal map from a topological space X to a compact Hausdorff space βX. The Stone–Čech compactification βX of a topological space X is the largest, most general compact Hausdorff space "generated" by X, in the sense that any continuous map from X to a compact Hausdorff space factors through βX (in a unique way). If X is a Tychonoff space then the map from X to its image in βX is a homeomorphism, so X can be thought of as a (dense) subspace of βX; every other compact Hausdorff space that densely contains X is a quotient of βX. For general topological spaces X, the map from X to βX need not be injective.

A form of the axiom of choice is required to prove that every topological space has a Stone–Čech compactification. Even for quite simple spaces X, an accessible concrete description of βX often remains elusive. In particular, proofs that βX \ X is nonempty do not give an explicit description of any particular point in βX \ X.

The Stone–Čech compactification occurs implicitly in a paper by Andrey Nikolayevich Tychonoff (1930) and was given explicitly by Marshall Stone (1937) and Eduard Čech (1937).
Raymond Duchamp-Villon (1876–1918) was a French Cubist sculptor known for *The Large Horse* and his contributions to modern art.

The Raccoon River is a 30.8-mile-long (49.6 km) tributary of the Des Moines River in central Iowa in the United States. As measured using the longest of its three forks, its length increases to 226 miles (364 km). Via the Des Moines River, it is part of the watershed of the Mississippi River. The river runs through an intensely cultivated area of croplands and livestock farming, receiving Tile drainage from slow-draining rich natural bottomland.

The Des Moines metropolitan area has been obtaining its drinking water from the Raccoon River just before it empties into the Des Moines River through water utilities since the 19th century. During the Great Flood of 1993, the Raccoon River flooded the water treatment facility of Des Moines, shutting off the city's supply of drinking water.

Beatrice Wishart (born 1955 or 1956) is a Scottish Liberal Democrat politician serving as the Member of the Scottish Parliament (MSP) for Shetland since 2019. Previously a Shetland Islands Council member, she won the 2019 Shetland by-election and was re-elected in 2021. Wishart serves as the party's education spokesperson and has been a member of several parliamentary committees, including those on education and COVID-19. She has advocated for education and regional strategies during the COVID-19 pandemic.

The Venezuelan coup attempt of November 1992 was an attempt to seize control of the government of Venezuela that took place on 27 November 1992. group of young military officers who were loyal to the Hugo Chávez-led Revolutionary Bolivarian Movement-200 (MBR-200), while Chávez was in prison for the February 1992 coup d'état attempt.

Pottawattamie Park is a small town in LaPorte County, Indiana, United States. Incorporated in 1936 on land once inhabited by the Potawatomi people, it spans 0.25 square miles and is entirely surrounded by Michigan City. As of the 2020 census, the town had a population of 242, with a predominantly residential character and a median age of 53.1 years.

The Bridge for Kids, officially known as the Orting Emergency Evacuation Bridge, is a proposed pedestrian suspension bridge in Orting, Washington, designed to provide rapid evacuation in the event of a lahar from Mount Rainier. Spanning 540 feet (160 m) across the Carbon River, the bridge would accommodate 12,000 people within a 40-minute evacuation window. Its design includes uneven footings and a steep slope, addressing the high lahar risk in the region's floodplain.

Benjamin Brackett Downing (born September 11, 1981) is an American politician and former Democratic member of the Massachusetts Senate, representing the Berkshire region from 2007 to 2017. During his tenure, he chaired several committees and focused on energy, revenue, and public service. After leaving office, he worked in renewable energy and briefly ran for Governor of Massachusetts in 2022.
*Extracts from Music for White Cube, London 1997* is a 1997 ambient album by Brian Eno, created for an installation at London's White Cube gallery.

The Reformed Christian Church in Slovakia is a Protestant denomination with roots in the Reformation of the 1520s, influenced by Calvinism. It has approximately 110,000 members, primarily Hungarian-speaking, and operates schools and mission congregations.

Oswald Chettle Mazengarb CBE QC (31 May 1890 – 27 November 1963) was a New Zealand barrister, King's Counsel, and politician. He served briefly on the New Zealand Legislative Council in 1950 as part of the "suicide squad" that voted for its abolition. Mazengarb was known for his legal expertise, authoring legal textbooks, and his public service, including chairing the 1954 Special Committee on Moral Delinquency in Children and Adolescents, which produced the influential Mazengarb Report. He was appointed a Commander of the Order of the British Empire in 1953.

The CMM 400-2 is a diesel multiple unit produced by Materfer in Córdoba Province, Argentina. The units are produced in Argentina's three primary track gauges and the standard gauge variants were the first to be produced for General Urquiza Railway services in Entre Ríos Province.

Latastia is a genus of lizards of the family Lacertidae. Species of this genus are distributed in Africa (Egypt, Ethiopia, Djibouti, Eritrea, Guinea-Bissau, Cameroon, Kenya, Malawi, Mali, Mozambique, Niger, Zambia, Senegal, Zimbabwe, Somalia, Sudan, South Sudan, Tanzania) but one subspecies (Latastia longicaudata andersonii) lives in Yemen. Collectively, they are known as long-tailed lizards.
Franziska Junge (born 1981 in Zschopau, East Germany) is a German actor and singer known for her work in theatre, film, and television.

City Tower (formerly Sunley House) is a 30-storey skyscraper situated in the Piccadilly Gardens area of Manchester city centre in England. As of 2022, it is the tallest office building in Manchester and outside London, and the eighth-tallest building in Manchester, with a roof height of 107 metres (351 ft).

Valmiera is a historic city in northern Latvia, located on the banks of the Gauja River. First mentioned in 1323, it has roots dating back over 9,000 years, with significant development during the medieval period as a member of the Hanseatic League. The city endured destruction during the Livonian War, Great Northern War, and World War II but emerged as an industrial and cultural hub. Today, Valmiera is known for its fiberglass production, educational institutions, and sports achievements, including its connection to Olympic athletes. It is part of the UNESCO Global Network of Learning Cities and hosts extensive sports infrastructure.

Jefferson Andrés Lerma Solís (born 25 October 1994) is a Colombian professional footballer who plays as a defensive midfielder for Premier League club AFC Bournemouth and the Colombia national team. Lerma began his career with Atlético Huila in Colombia, where he made his professional debut in 2013 and became a key player. In 2015, he joined Spanish club Levante, initially on loan, and helped them achieve promotion to La Liga during the 2016–17 season. 

In 2018, Lerma signed with Bournemouth for a club-record fee, becoming a fan favorite for his tough-tackling style. He played a pivotal role in Bournemouth's promotion back to the Premier League in the 2021–22 season. Internationally, Lerma has represented Colombia since 2017, featuring in the 2018 FIFA World Cup and the 2019 Copa América. Known for his versatility and physicality, he has also scored key goals for both club and country.
Konstantin Ivanov Slavchev (Lang-bg) also known as Konstantin (Lang-bg) is a Bulgarian pop-folk singer. He was the winner of the Bulgarian reality show VIP Brother 1 in 2006.

Peter Friedrich Röding (17 June 1767 – 8 June 1846) was a German malacologist who lived in Hamburg. Very little is known about this naturalist.

Many of Röding's descriptions (often simply a German rendition of the Latin binomial name) are of species which were first named by earlier authors such as Johann Hieronymus Chemnitz, Friedrich Wilhelm Martini and Martin Lister. Röding's references to pre-existing descriptions and figures make these names also valid, since they are unequivocally recognizable, and were (after Röding) subsequently adopted by many later authors.
Stevan Mirković (27 October 1927 – 26 September 2015) was a Yugoslav Colonel General, Chief of the General Staff (1987–1989), and World War II veteran.

Pottawattamie Park is a town in Michigan Township, LaPorte County, Indiana, United States. The population was 235 at the 2010 census. It is included in the Michigan City, Indiana-La Porte, Indiana Metropolitan Statistical Area.

The January 1993 airstrikes on Iraq were a series of military operations conducted by the United States, United Kingdom, and France in response to Iraqi violations of no-fly zones established after the Gulf War. Spanning January 13–22, the strikes targeted surface-to-air missile sites, radar stations, and other military infrastructure in southern and northern Iraq. The operations involved extensive coalition airpower, including U.S. Navy and Air Force aircraft, as well as British and French forces. While some targets were successfully destroyed, the results were mixed, with several strikes missing their objectives. The airstrikes marked continued enforcement of no-fly zones and heightened tensions with Iraq during the transition from the George H. W. Bush to Bill Clinton administrations. Casualties included Iraqi military personnel and civilians.
Frederick Eugene Crawford (July 27, 1910 – March 5, 1974) was an American football player.

The Maquis des Glières was a Free French Resistance group, which fought against the 1940–1944 German occupation of France in World War II. The name is also given to the military conflict that opposed Resistance fighters to German, Vichy and Milice forces.

"Love Theme from The Godfather" is a musical piece composed by Nino Rota for the 1972 film *The Godfather*, with original English lyrics by Larry Kusik. The theme, adapted from Rota's earlier work for the 1958 film *Fortunella*, has been recorded in multiple languages and versions, including "Speak Softly Love." While Rota's score was disqualified from the 1973 Academy Awards, it won for *The Godfather Part II*. The theme achieved chart success through various renditions.

The medieval University of Dublin (Lang-la) was an early but generally unsuccessful attempt to establish a university in Dublin, the capital city of the Lordship of Ireland. Founded in 1320, it maintained an intermittent existence for the next two centuries, but it never flourished, and disappeared for good at the Reformation in Ireland (1534–41). It was located in Saint Patrick's Cathedral, Dublin. It had no connection with the present University of Dublin, better known as Trinity College Dublin, which was founded in 1592.
