Set in the midst of World War II, Day of Defeat includes no single-player campaign, with focus left only on the game's multiplayer aspects. The game favors teamwork and features objective-based gameplay in combination with its system of classes. Maps are primarily made up of narrow paths, all of which typically lead to a few key locations. An official remake of the game, Day of Defeat: Source, was released by Valve in 2005.

St John's Rectory, located at 3 Vicar's Lane in Chester, Cheshire, England, is a Georgian-style building constructed around 1750 as the rectory for St John's Church. It is a Grade II* listed building, noted for its symmetrical brick design with stone dressings, sash windows, and an ornate entrance. The rectory served its original purpose until 1957, after which it was used by the Grosvenor Club and later converted into offices.

Michelle O'Bonsawin (born 1973 or 1974) is a Canadian jurist serving as a puisne justice of the Supreme Court of Canada since September 1, 2022. Appointed by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, she is the first Indigenous person to serve on the court. A Franco-Ontarian and Abenaki member of the Odanak First Nation, O'Bonsawin previously served on the Ontario Superior Court of Justice.

Todd Compton is an American historian, author, and independent researcher specializing in Mormon history and classical studies. A member of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, he is best known for his award-winning book *In Sacred Loneliness: The Plural Wives of Joseph Smith*. Compton has also published extensively on Mormon history, classical mythology, and popular culture, contributing to journals such as *Dialogue: A Journal of Mormon Thought* and *Sunstone Magazine*. His biography of Jacob Hamblin, *A Frontier Life*, received multiple awards, and he has served on editorial boards for prominent Mormon history publications.

Amy Yamada (山田詠美, born February 8, 1959) is a Japanese novelist, short-story writer, and essayist known for her exploration of themes such as race, identity, and relationships. She gained widespread recognition with her 1985 novel *Bedtime Eyes*, which won the Bungei Prize and drew on her experiences with African-American culture. Her works, including *Soul Music Lovers Only* and *Trash*, often address issues of separation, bullying, and childhood struggles. Yamada's writing has influenced contemporary Japanese authors and has been translated into English.

Stanton Peele is an American psychologist, attorney, and addiction specialist known for his critique of the disease model of addiction and his experiential approach to understanding addictive behaviors. He has authored 14 books, including *Love and Addiction* (1975) and *Diseasing of America* (1989), and developed the Life Process Program, a non-12-step addiction treatment model. Peele argues that addiction stems from life circumstances rather than being a medical condition, advocating for self-efficacy and socialization as key to overcoming addiction. His work has been influential in challenging traditional addiction treatment paradigms.

Kogiidae is a family comprising at least two extant species of Cetacea, the pygmy (Kogia breviceps) and dwarf (K. sima) sperm whales. As their common names suggest, they somewhat resemble sperm whales, with squared heads and small lower jaws, but are much smaller in size, with much shorter skulls and more notable dorsal fins than sperm whales. Kogiids are also characterized by a "false gill slit" behind their eyes.

A number of genera are extinct.

The discography of Face to Face, an American punk rock band formed in 1991, includes 10 studio albums, 8 EPs, 3 live albums, 2 compilation albums, 12 singles, and 11 music videos. The band gained prominence with their debut album *Don't Turn Away* (1992) and achieved further success with *Big Choice* (1995), featuring the hit "Disconnected." After releasing several albums and undergoing lineup changes, the band disbanded in 2004 but reunited in 2008. They have since continued to perform and release new music, including their 2011 comeback album *Laugh Now, Laugh Later.*

Artillerie-Inrichtingen was a Dutch state-owned artillery, small arms, and munitions company which also produced machine tools and was founded in 1679 in Delft, Netherlands. The company was split in 1973 with its defense related businesses becoming Eurometaal and its civilian manufacturing becoming Hembrug Machine Tools. During its years of operation as Artillerie Inrichtingen, the company manufactured armaments and an array of other industrial outputs for the Dutch Army and the Royal Dutch East Indies Army (KNIL).

The Stockade Building System was designed by Richard Buckminster Fuller and his father-in-law, James Monroe Hewlett, and was patented in 1927. Both of them had previously formed a company, in 1922, which made bricks out of compressed wood shavings with vertical holes cast in them.

Jeff Danielson (born August 6, 1970) is a former Democratic Iowa State Senator. Having served the 30th Senate District between 2013 and 2019, he had also previously served the 10th Senate District up until redistricting, between 2005 and 2013.

Danielson served on following committees: Appropriations (Vice Chair), Economic Growth, State Government (Chair), Transportation, Veterans Affairs (Vice Chair), and the Administration and Regulation Appropriations Subcommittee (Vice Chair). Permanent statutory committees he serves on include: Public Retirement Systems and State Government Efficiency Review. Danielson additionally serves on the Iowa Capital Investment Board.

His prior public service experience includes experience as the Commissioner of the Iowa Department of Transportation and the Waterloo Planning, Programming, and Zoning Commission. He is a U.S. Navy veteran and serves the community of Cedar Falls as a professional firefighter.

Courtney Andrew Walsh OJ (born 30 October 1962) is a former Jamaican cricketer who represented the West Indies from 1984 to 2001, captaining the West Indies in 22 Test matches. He is a fast bowler and considered one of the all time greats, best known for a remarkable opening bowling partnership along with fellow West Indian Curtly Ambrose for several years. Walsh played 132 Tests and 205 ODIs for the West Indies and took 519 and 227 wickets respectively. He shared 421 Test wickets with Ambrose in 49 matches. He held the record of most Test wickets from 2000, after he broke the record of Kapil Dev. This record was later broken in 2004 by Shane Warne. He was the first bowler to reach 500 wickets in Test cricket. His autobiography is entitled "Heart of the Lion". Walsh was named one of the Wisden Cricketers of the Year in 1987. In October 2010, he was inducted into the ICC Cricket Hall of Fame. He was appointed as the Specialist Bowling Coach of Bangladesh Cricket Team in August 2016.

*La Spinalba*, also known as *La Spinalba, ovvero Il vecchio matto*, is a three-act opera by Portuguese composer Francisco António de Almeida. Composed in the 1730s, it is the only one of Almeida's operas to survive intact, showcasing influences from Giovanni Battista Pergolesi and Alessandro Scarlatti. Rediscovered in the 1960s after being presumed lost in the 1755 Lisbon earthquake, the opera received its first modern performance in 1965 at the Teatro Nacional de São Carlos in Lisbon. It has since been performed internationally, including in London and by the Guildhall School of Music and Drama. The opera features a comedic and dramatic plot involving themes of disguise, mistaken identity, and romantic entanglements, culminating in reconciliation and resolution. A studio recording by Os Músicos do Tejo was released in 2012, further reviving interest in Almeida's work.

The *Joseph Conrad* is a historic full-rigged sailing ship built in 1882 in Denmark as *Georg Stage*. Renamed by Australian sailor Alan Villiers in 1934, it completed a global circumnavigation before serving as a training ship for the U.S. Merchant Marine during World War II. Since 1947, it has been a museum and training vessel at Mystic Seaport in Connecticut.

George Washington's reception at Trenton was a celebration hosted by the Ladies of Trenton social club on April 21, 1789, in Trenton, New Jersey, as George Washington, then president-elect, journeyed from his home at Mount Vernon to his first inauguration in the then capital of the United States, New York City. A ceremonial triumphal arch was erected on the bridge over the Assunpink Creek to commemorate his two victories here, the Battle of Trenton on December 26, 1776 and the Battle of the Assunpink Creek on January 2, 1777.

The Geologist is an glue paint on paper on canvas painting by the German painter Carl Spitzweg executed c. 1860. It belongs to the collection of the Von der Heydt Museum in Wuppertal, to which it was bequeathed in 1913. Spitzweg's style of painting belongs to the late Romantic period.
The women's hockey tournament at the 2010 Commonwealth Games featured teams from 10 nations, each led by a head coach, with squad announcements made prior to the event.
Hugo Nathan was a German banker, art collector, and victim of Nazi-era persecution.

The *Christmas in Spiceworld Tour* was a 1999 concert tour by the Spice Girls, held exclusively in the United Kingdom from December 4 to December 15. Featuring eight shows, the tour marked the group's return after a hiatus, showcasing an elaborate stage design with Christmas-themed elements. The performances were divided into three sections, blending hits, new songs, and festive medleys. A full concert at Earl's Court was broadcast live on Sky One, including behind-the-scenes footage. The tour was marred by the death of a crew member during set dismantling.

Jefferson Andrés Lerma Solís (born 25 October 1994) is a Colombian professional footballer who plays as a central midfielder for Premier League club AFC Bournemouth and the Colombia national team. He has also been used as a defensive midfielder and as a right-back.

A youth product of Atlético Huila, Lerma played for three years in Colombia before joining Spanish side Levante on loan in 2016. That same year, Levante bought Lerma outright on a four-year contract. Lerma became Premier League side AFC Bournemouth's club record transfer fee in 2018 as he joined The Cherries for a reported €30 million.

Lerma first played with Colombia's Olympic team, representing them at the 2016 Summer Olympics in Rio. He made his international debut for Colombia the following year in a friendly against South Korea. He was part of the Colombia squad that participated at the 2018 World Cup, and represented his nation at the 2019 edition of the Copa América.

The former Reading Municipal Building is a historic building at 49 Pleasant Street in Reading, Massachusetts. Built in 1885, this two-story brick building was the town's first municipal structure, housing the town offices, jail, and fire station. In 1918 all functions except fire services moved out of the building. It now serves as Reading's Pleasant Street Senior Center. The building was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1984.

Pierre Galet (1921 – 31 December 2019) was a Monegasque-born French ampelographer and viticulturist, widely regarded as a pioneer in the field of grape identification and classification. He spent much of his career at the University of Montpellier, where he taught from 1946 to 1989 and mentored prominent ampelographers, including Paul Truel. Galet traveled extensively, identifying grape varieties and resolving legal disputes, such as those involving European Union regulations on American grapevines. He identified over 100 Pinot family varieties and corrected misidentifications, including a notable case in California involving Melon de Bourgogne.

Galet authored several influential works, including *Cépages et vignoble de France* and *Dictionnaire encyclopédique des cépages*. For his contributions, he was named an Officier de l'Ordre du Mérite Agricole and received recognition from the French vintners' association.

Yvette Paula Fielding (born 23 September 1968) is an English television presenter, producer, actress, and author, best known as the youngest-ever presenter of *Blue Peter* and as the host of the paranormal investigation series *Most Haunted*. Beginning her career in 1983 with the BBC series *Seaview*, she gained prominence on *Blue Peter* from 1987 to 1992, earning widespread recognition. Fielding later transitioned to adult television, hosting various shows before co-founding Antix Productions and creating *Most Haunted* in 2002. She has also authored books, including *The Ghost Hunter Chronicles* series, and lives with her family in Cheshire.

Andrée Geulen (6 September 1921 – 31 May 2022) was a Belgian teacher and member of the resistance during World War II, known for her work with the Committee for the Defence of Jews (CDJ) in Belgium. She helped rescue and hide around 300 Jewish children, placing them with Catholic families and institutions under false identities to protect them from Nazi persecution. After the war, she worked to reunite hidden children with their families and supported Jewish survivors. Recognized as Righteous Among the Nations in 1989, she received honorary Israeli citizenship in 2007. Geulen remained an advocate for pacifist and anti-racist causes throughout her life.

Kylie Nicole Sicangco Padilla (born January 25, 1993) is a Filipino actress, singer, and model. Known for her roles in *Encantadia* (2016) and *Joaquin Bordado*, she is a GMA Network contract star and a recipient of the 2008 PMPC Star Award for Best New Female Personality. Born to actors Robin Padilla and Liezl Sicangco, she spent her early years in Australia and practices Islam. Padilla was married to actor Aljur Abrenica, with whom she has two children, before their separation in 2021.
Rose Venkatesan is an Indian talk show host, radio jockey, politician, and LGBTQ activist. Known for hosting *Ippadikku Rose*, she advocates for sexual freedom, gender rights, and societal reform.

*Unleashed* (released as *Danny the Dog* in France) is a 2005 action-thriller film directed by Louis Leterrier and written by Luc Besson. Starring Jet Li, Morgan Freeman, Bob Hoskins, and Kerry Condon, the film follows a man raised as a human attack dog who discovers humanity through music and family. Known for its gritty fight sequences and emotional depth, *Unleashed* received generally positive reviews and grossed $50.9 million worldwide. The soundtrack, composed by Massive Attack, was critically acclaimed.
Hugo Nathan (1861-1921) was a German banker and art collector.

HMS *Modeste* was a 64-gun third-rate ship of the line originally built for the French Navy and launched in 1759. Captured by the Royal Navy during the Battle of Lagos in August 1759, she was commissioned as HMS *Modeste* in 1760. The ship served in the Mediterranean and the Caribbean, participating in notable actions such as the capture of Martinique in 1762. After periods of active service and being laid up, she was converted into a receiving ship in 1778 and remained in this role until being broken up in 1800.
STM (Savunma Teknolojileri Mühendislik ve Ticaret A.Ş.) is a company operating in Turkey, operating in the civil, public (including military) and private sectors.
Ham Green Pottery was produced between 1100 AD to 1250 AD at a hamlet above the village of Pill called Ham Green in the English county of Somerset.

Curtis Knight and the Squires were a New York band that was fronted by singer and guitarist Curtis Knight in the mid-1960s. Both Jimi Hendrix and sax player Lonnie Youngblood were members for a while.
Odostomia farallonensis is a species of sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusc in the family Pyramidellidae, the pyrams and their allies.

Bajrang Punia (born 26 February 1994) is an Indian freestyle wrestler and Olympic medalist. He has won multiple medals at prestigious international events, including a bronze at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, silver at the 2018 World Wrestling Championships, and gold at the 2018 Asian Games and 2022 Commonwealth Games. Known for his dominance in the 65 kg category, Punia has also claimed numerous Asian and Commonwealth titles. Born in Khudan, Haryana, he began wrestling at a young age, inspired by his father, and trained at Chhatrasal Stadium. Punia is married to fellow wrestler Sangeeta Phogat and works as a Gazetted Officer in the Indian Railways.
Extracts from Music for White Cube, London 1997 is the seventeenth solo studio album from British musician Brian Eno, released in 1997.

Shanghai Street is a 2.3 km long street in the Jordan, Yau Ma Tei and Mong Kok areas of Kowloon, Hong Kong, China. Completed in 1887 under the name of Station Street (差館街), it was once the most prosperous street in Kowloon. It originates from the south at Austin Road, and terminates in the north at Lai Chi Kok Road. Parallel to Shanghai Street are Nathan Road, Temple Street, Portland Street, Reclamation Street and Canton Road. Though parallel, Shanghai Street was marked by 2- to 3-floor Chinese-style buildings while Nathan Road was marked by Western-style buildings.

John Patrick (born February 29, 1968) is an American basketball coach and former player, currently serving as head coach of the Chiba Jets Funabashi in Japan's B.League. A Stanford University alumnus, he played professionally for 13 years in Japan and Germany before transitioning to coaching in 2003. Patrick is known for his success in European basketball, including leading BG Göttingen to the 2010 FIBA EuroChallenge title and earning multiple Coach of the Year honors in Germany's Basketball Bundesliga. He also coached MHP Riesen Ludwigsburg to several playoff appearances and a Champions League bronze medal.
Academic ranks in the United States are the titles, relative importance and power of professors, researchers, and administrative personnel held in academia.

The Bridge for Kids is a proposed bridge across the Carbon River in Orting, Washington, about a mile upstream of where it joins the Puyallup River. It would provide an emergency evacuation route for school children to escape a future lahar flow from Mount Rainier, consisting of an up to 10-meter (33 ft) high flood of mud, rock and boulders. As of 2016, the $40 million bridge was still in the planning phase.

Ryan Higa (born June 6, 1990), also known as nigahiga (/ˈniːɡəhiːɡə/ NEE-ɡə-HEE-ɡə), is an American Internet personality. Best known for his comedy videos on YouTube, Higa began making YouTube videos in 2006 and was one of the most popular creators on the platform in its early years. His main YouTube channel, nigahiga, was the most subscribed channel on YouTube for 677 consecutive days from 2009 to 2011, an achievement that has only been surpassed by PewDiePie and T-Series. Higa was the first person to reach the milestones of 2 million and 3 million subscribers on YouTube. Higa launched a podcast in 2018 called Off the Pill, which has featured YouTubers and celebrities such as KevJumba, Andrew Yang, and Jeremy Lin. In 2020, Higa started streaming on Twitch, where he reacts to his past videos and broadcasts video game content, most notably in Valorant.

As of November 2022, he has over 21 million subscribers on YouTube and 756,000 followers on Twitch. Higa has won a Shorty Award, has been named Forbes Top 30 Under 30, and has been nominated for three more Shorty Awards, six Streamy Awards, and five Teen Choice Awards.

Outside of his content on YouTube and Twitch, he has also published a memoir Ryan Higa's How to Write Good and appeared in feature films Tell Me How I Die (2016) and Finding'Ohana (2021).

Basuki Tjahaja Purnama (Chinese: 鐘萬學; pinyin: Zhōng Wànxué, Pha̍k-fa-sṳ: Chûng Van-ho̍k; born 29 June 1966) is an Indonesian businessman, politician, and former governor of Jakarta. He is also known by his Hakka Chinese nickname Ahok (Chinese: 阿學). He was the second minority governor of Jakarta with Chinese ancestry and also of the Evangelical faith, following Henk Ngantung, who was governor of Catholic faith from 1964–65.

Ahok was a legislator in the Indonesian People's Representative Council (DPR) and Regent of East Belitung. He was elected to the House of Representatives for the 2009–2014 term but resigned in 2012 to run for the deputy governorship of Jakarta, to which he was elected. In November 2014, he became governor of Jakarta, as his predecessor Joko Widodo had become president. Ahead of the 2017 Jakarta gubernatorial election, Ahok's political rivals aligned themselves with Islamic extremists to exploit religious and racial intolerance, resulting in Ahok being accused of blasphemy in October 2016. He then lost the election to former Education Minister Anies Baswedan and was imprisoned for blasphemy.

Aurélien Bayard Chedjou Fongang (IPA-fr; born 20 June 1985) is a Cameroonian former professional footballer who played as a centre back for LOSC Lille, Galatasaray, Bursaspor, Adana Demirspor and the Cameroon national team.

Frank Cherry (born in the Southern United States) was a religious leader and founder of the Church of the Living God, the Pillar Ground of Truth for All Nations, established in 1886. He preached that African Americans were descendants of the ancient Israelites, espoused Black Nationalism, and supported Marcus Garvey. Cherry also promoted controversial beliefs, including antisemitism, the inherent evil of white people, and the idea that the earth is square. After his death, his son Prince Benjamin F. Cherry succeeded him as the church's leader.

The Gao Empire was a prominent West African state that emerged around the 9th century and lasted until 1430, when it was succeeded by the Songhai Empire. Centered in the city of Gao along the Niger River, the empire became a vital hub for trade, fishing, and manufacturing, with its craftspeople producing highly valued carnelian beads. Gao's strategic location contributed to its prosperity and regional influence, particularly during the early Islamic period when it became a center of Muslim culture and governance.

The empire's history is primarily documented through external Arabic sources, as indigenous written records are scarce. Archaeological evidence suggests the existence of two settlements, Gao Ancien and Gao-Saney, which played significant roles in the empire's development. The introduction of Islam to Gao occurred gradually, with the Zuwa dynasty adopting the religion by the late 11th century, possibly influenced by the Almoravids.

Gao's decline began in the late 13th century when it was absorbed into the Mali Empire. However, it regained independence in the 15th century under Sunni rulers, eventually becoming the foundation for the Songhai Empire, which expanded Gao's legacy.

The Trochilinae are a subfamily of hummingbirds within the family Trochilidae, comprising three tribes: Lampornithini (mountain gems), Mellisugini (bees), and Trochilini (emeralds). Their classification was redefined based on molecular phylogenetic studies.

Trochilinae is one of the six subfamilies that make up the hummingbird family Trochilidae.

The subfamily is divided into three tribes: Lampornithini (mountain gems) containing 18 species, Mellisugini (bees) containing 37 species and Trochilini (emeralds) containing 115 species.

Al-Majd (Arabic: خربة المجد) is a Palestinian village in the Hebron Governorate, located in the southern West Bank. Governed by a village council, it had a population of 1,925 in 2007. The village has historical significance, with evidence of Byzantine-era ceramics and remnants of a ruined chapel. During the Ottoman era, it was noted for its caves, cisterns, and pillar shafts. Following the 1948 Arab–Israeli War, Al-Majd came under Jordanian rule until the Six-Day War in 1967, after which it has been under Israeli occupation.
Crossing the Red Sea with The Adverts is the debut studio album by English punk rock band the Adverts. It was released on 17 February 1978 by record label Bright.
Cardinal Newman High School is a Catholic school in Columbia, South Carolina, serving grades 7–12. Established in 1858 by the Ursuline Sisters, it moved to a 50-acre campus in 2016.
Uptown New York is a 1932 American Pre-Code film directed by Victor Schertzinger and starring Jack Oakie, Shirley Grey, and Leon Ames. It is based on the story by Vina Delmar.
Stevie Anderson (born May 12, 1970) is a former American football wide receiver who played in the NFL from 1993 to 1997.

A chase gun was a naval cannon used during the Age of Sail, mounted at the bow or stern of a ship to fire directly ahead or behind during a pursuit. These guns were employed in prolonged chases, where ships fine-tuned their sails to exploit wind conditions. Chase guns, often made of brass for improved accuracy, were used to target an enemy ship's rigging or critical lines, with the aim of slowing or disabling the vessel. Despite their purpose, they were generally of limited accuracy, as demonstrated by an eighteenth-century British crew that required seventy-two shots to hit an enemy's sails.

By the late eighteenth century, the Royal Navy enhanced the effectiveness of chase guns through improved artillery training and modifications to gun carriages for greater range and elevation. From 1799, Royal Navy frigates were equipped with standardized bow and stern chasers for use in pursuit scenarios.
Steve White (born 2 January 1959 in Chipping Sodbury) is a retired professional football forward and manager.
Scott Campbell (born December 28, 1973) is an American artist, production designer, and art director known for his work on video games, comics, and gallery exhibitions.

Nuvilly is a municipality in the district of Broye, canton of Fribourg, Switzerland. First mentioned in 1242, it spans 3.98 km² and is primarily agricultural, with a population of 471 as of 2020. The village features a baroque-style Catholic church, consecrated in 1690 and dedicated to James the Great, which underwent restoration in 2006–2007. Known for its scenic countryside, Nuvilly offers hiking trails and nature sightseeing opportunities.

Dehraadun Diary is a Hindi mystery thriller film released in January 2013. This was the debut film for Ragini Nandwani who made the jump from TV to film industry. The film is based on a real life murder.
Rod Bradley (born 29 March 1983) is an English player of American football, as well an artist and entrepreneur.

Purcari, part of the Purcari Wineries LLC (pronunciation: [purˈkarj]), is a popular wine brand in Central and Eastern Europe. The Winery is located in Purcari Wine Region, Ștefan Vodă District. Through the years, Purcari Winery has won over 250 medals at international contests like Decanter World Wine Awards, International Wine & Spirits Competition, International Wine Challenge and many other competitions.

As an exporter, Purcari Winery has entered in over 30 markets in Europe, North America, and East Asia, in countries like the US, Canada, Great Britain, Norway, Czech Republic, China, Poland, Germany, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Ukraine, Romania, Turkey, Japan, Luxembourg, and in the Baltic Countries.

The Government of Republic of Moldova awarded Purcari Winery with the Grand Prix for achievements in wine making.
The 2022–23 season was AS Saint-Étienne's first in Ligue 2 following relegation from Ligue 1. Led by head coach Laurent Batlles, the team competed in Ligue 2 and the Coupe de France.
Papua New Guinea competed at the 2016 Summer Paralympics in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, from 7 September to 18 September 2016.

The 2021 Little League World Series was held from August 19 to August 29 in South Williamsport, Pennsylvania, featuring 16 U.S.-based teams due to COVID-19 restrictions. The tournament was divided into two brackets, the Tom Seaver and Hank Aaron championship brackets. Taylor North Little League from Taylor, Michigan, won the championship, defeating West Side Little League from Hamilton, Ohio, 5–2 in the final, a rematch of the Great Lakes regional final. Ella Bruning of Abilene, Texas, became one of the few girls to compete in the series. The event also included the 2021 MLB Little League Classic, where the Cleveland Indians defeated the Los Angeles Angels 3–0. This was the fourth edition of the Classic and the first between American League teams.

John Patrick Rourke (born 26 March 1942) is a South African botanist known for his work on the Cape flora and the Proteaceae family. He served as curator of the Compton Herbarium at Kirstenbosch National Botanical Garden and published significant revisions of Proteacean genera. Rourke has collected approximately 2,000 plant specimens and received numerous honors, including the Linnean Society of London foreign membership in 1997 and the Cape Tercentenary Foundation's Gold Medal in 2003. Several plant species have been named in his honor.

An avulsion injury occurs when tissue is forcibly detached from its normal position, ranging in severity from minor skin flaps to complete amputations. Common types include skin avulsions, often caused by motor vehicle collisions, and nail avulsions, where the nail is torn from the nail bed. Specific forms include ear, eyelid, and tooth avulsions, as well as brachial plexus avulsions, which affect nerve function. Treatment varies by type and severity, involving sutures, grafts, or surgical reattachment. Avulsions can also be performed surgically to address medical conditions, such as varicose veins or ingrown nails.

The President's Scholarship is Singapore's most prestigious undergraduate scholarship, awarded to outstanding students who demonstrate exceptional academic performance, leadership qualities, and a commitment to serving the nation. It traces its origins to the Queen's Scholarship, established in 1885 by Cecil Clementi Smith, then Governor of the Straits Settlements. The Queen's Scholarship was replaced by the Singapore State Scholarship in 1959 following Singapore's attainment of self-governance. In 1964, the Yang di-Pertuan Negara Scholarship succeeded the State Scholarship, and it was renamed the President's Scholarship in 1966 after Singapore's independence.

Recipients of the President's Scholarship often go on to hold prominent positions in Singapore's civil service. Schools with the highest number of President's Scholars among their alumni have been identified through data collected by *The Straits Times*, reflecting the scholarship's role in shaping the nation's leadership.

The *Testamentum Domini* is an early Christian text, likely composed in the 4th or 5th century CE, falsely attributed to Jesus Christ as instructions to his Apostles. Written originally in Greek, the text survives in Syriac, Latin, and other translations. It is part of the Clementine Octateuch and combines apocalyptic elements, church regulations, and ascetic doctrine.

"Love Theme from The Godfather" is an instrumental theme from the 1972 film The Godfather, composed by Nino Rota. The piece was lyricized in English by Larry Kusik into "Speak Softly, Love", a popular song released in 1972. The highest-charting rendition of either version was by vocalist Andy Williams, who took "Speak Softly Love" to number 34 on Billboard magazine's Hot 100 and number seven on its Easy Listening chart.

Oak is a discontinued programming language created by James Gosling in 1989, initially for Sun Microsystems' set-top box project. The language later evolved to become Java.

The name Oak was used by Gosling after an oak tree that stood outside his office.

Powers Auditorium, in Youngstown, Ohio is one of the largest auditoriums in the Youngstown-Warren area. The facility is the main venue of downtown Youngstown's DeYor Performing Arts Center. The complex also includes the Adler Art Academy, Beecher Flad Pavilion, and Ford Family Recital Hall. Originally built in 1931 as the Warner Theatre, the former movie palace was renovated and reopened as Powers Auditorium in 1969.

The main tenant of Powers Auditorium is the Youngstown Symphony, which performs from October through May. The facility also hosts other musical acts, touring Broadway productions, and locally produced theater (primarily from Ballet Western Reserve and Easy Street Productions, two Youngstown-based theater companies).

Hristo Chernopeev (Lang-bg) (1868, Dermantsi – 6 November 1915, Krivolak) was a Bulgarian Army officer and member of the revolutionary movement in Macedonia. He was among the leaders of the Bulgarian People's Macedonian-Adrianople Revolutionary Organization.

The Silver Jubilee Independence Cup was a One Day International cricket tournament held in Dhaka, Bangladesh during January 1998. The tournament was held as a celebration of 25 years of Bangladesh's independence and all the games were held at Bangabandhu National Stadium, Dhaka, Bangladesh.India, Pakistan and the hosts Bangladesh were the participating teams in the tournament.

India were the winners of the tournament after beating Pakistan in the third final of the best of three finals. India successfully chased Pakistan's total of 314/5 in a match that, at the time, was a world record for the highest successful run chase in One Day International cricket. Hrishikesh Kanitkar hit a four (Video)when 3 runs were required off the last two balls to help India chase down the record target and lift the Silver Jubilee Independence Cup.

Saurav Ganguly was awarded player of the match for his century in the third final, while Sachin Tendulkar was named the player of the series.

Bernard Vince (born 2 October 1985) is a former Australian rules footballer who played as a midfielder for the Adelaide Football Club and Melbourne Football Club in the Australian Football League (AFL). Originally from Stansbury, South Australia, he was drafted by Adelaide in 2005 and won the Malcolm Blight Medal in 2009. After being traded to Melbourne in 2013, he won the Keith 'Bluey' Truscott Medal in 2015. Vince retired in 2018 and is married with one son.

*Iris variegata*, commonly known as the Hungarian iris, is a species of flowering plant in the family Iridaceae, native to central and southeastern Europe. It is characterized by its stout rhizomes, dark green ribbed leaves, and distinctive yellowish-white flowers with brown-purple veins, which bloom in early summer. The species is diploid with a chromosome count of 2n=24 and has played a significant role in the development of modern bearded iris hybrids. *Iris variegata* thrives in open, stony areas and is considered endangered in parts of its native range.

Gerard Alsteens (born 3 August 1940, in Auderghem) is a Belgian political cartoonist, graphic artist and painter who works under the pseudonym "GAL". which, artistically speaking, share a closer resemblance to paintings than regularly drawn cartoons. Throughout the years his work has received several prizes and awards.

Fred Crawford (July 27, 1910 – March 5, 1974) was an American football player, coach, and World War II veteran. A standout tackle and end for Duke University, he became the first football player from North Carolina to earn consensus All-America honors in 1933. Known for his exceptional play, he helped lead Duke to a Southern Conference championship and was later inducted into the College Football Hall of Fame in 1973. Crawford briefly played professionally for the Chicago Bears before serving in the United States Air Force during World War II. After the war, he worked for the Florida State Motor Vehicle Department until his death.
Perla is a Mexican telenovela produced by TV Azteca and starred Silvia Navarro and Leonardo Garcia as protagonists.

The 2021 Little League World Series was held from August 19 to August 29 at the Little League headquarters complex in South Williamsport, Pennsylvania. Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the event was limited to US-based teams only. The most recent prior edition of the tournament contested without international teams had been 1975. Taylor North Little League of Taylor, Michigan, defeated West Side Little League of Hamilton, Ohio, in the championship by a 5–2 score. It was the first championship for a team from Michigan since 1959.

On August 13, it was announced that tickets would not be distributed to the public due to concerns over the Delta variant, with spectators limited to invited friends and family of teams, and other high-level supporters.

Matthijs van Nieuwkerk (born 8 September 1960) is a Dutch journalist and television presenter, best known for hosting the popular talk show *De Wereld Draait Door* from 2005 to 2020. He began his career in journalism at *Het Parool*, where he became editor-in-chief in 1996, before transitioning to television as editor-in-chief of AT5 and later as a presenter for various national programs. Van Nieuwkerk also co-created and hosted shows like *Holland Sport*, *Popquiz*, and *Top 2000 à Go-Go*. In 2022, he faced accusations of workplace bullying during his time on *De Wereld Draait Door*. Beyond television, he has contributed to football magazine *Hard Gras* and written columns and books. Van Nieuwkerk divorced in 2021 and has two children.
Fatma Nur Serter (born 10 November 1948) is a female Turkish academic and politician.

Our Lady and St Peter's Church is a Roman Catholic parish church in Bridlington, England. Built in the Gothic Revival style, it was designed by Smith, Brodrick & Lowther and completed in 1894. The church, dedicated to Mary and Saint Peter, is Grade II listed and serves the Our Lady Star of the Sea parish alongside churches in Eastfield and Filey.

Qi Xin (simplified Chinese: 齐心; traditional Chinese: 齊心; born November 3, 1926) is a Chinese author and member of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP), who wrote various articles on her husband, Chinese communist revolutionary Xi Zhongxun. She is the mother of Xi Jinping, current General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party, also known as the paramount leader.

Paddleton is a 2019 American comedy-drama film, directed by Alex Lehmann, from a script by Lehmann and Mark Duplass. The film stars Duplass and Ray Romano.

The film had its world premiere at the Sundance Film Festival on February 1, 2019. It was released on February 22, 2019, by Netflix to generally favorable reviews from critics.

Alfred Lubbock (31 October 1845 – 17 July 1916) was an English cricketer and footballer. A right-handed batsman, he played 28 first-class cricket matches between 1863 and 1875, primarily for Kent County Cricket Club and the Marylebone Cricket Club (MCC). Known for his batting skill, he scored two centuries and was considered one of the best batsmen of his era. Lubbock also played football, appearing for Old Etonians in the 1875 FA Cup Final replay. He later worked as an underwriter and banker, authoring *Memories of Eton and Etonians* in 1899.
Sthenelaidas (Gr. Σθενελαίδας) was a Spartan who held the office of ephor in 432 BC, during which he played a critical role in starting the Peloponnesian War against Athens.

Peter Friedrich Röding (17 June 1767 – 8 June 1846) was a German malacologist known for his contributions to taxonomy through the 1798 publication *Museum Boltenianum*, a catalogue of mollusc specimens from the collection of Joachim Friedrich Bolten. Initially overlooked as a taxonomic work, the catalogue was later recognized by William Healey Dall for introducing valid taxa, leading to Röding being credited with naming numerous mollusc genera. The work, published anonymously, was officially attributed to Röding by the International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature in 1956.

Poot Mae Nam Khong (Lang-th, RTGS: Phut Mae Nam Khong, literally The Spirit of Mekong River) is a Thai Horror Superstition Lakorn, remade from 1990 lakorn of the same name. The lakorn starring by Weir Sukollawat and Jui Warattaya as the Main roles and Morakot Aimee Kittisara as the ghost.

Utría National Natural Park (Parque Nacional Natural Utría) is a protected area located on the Pacific coast of Colombia in the Chocó Department. Established in October 1987, the park spans 54,300 hectares (210 square miles) and features diverse ecosystems, including mangroves, coral reefs, tropical forests, and the Utría Cove, a large seawater lagoon. It is home to a wide variety of flora and fauna, such as humpback whales, sea turtles, jaguars, and over 380 bird species. The park overlaps with the territories of the Emberá indigenous people and Afro-Colombian fishing communities, who contribute to its conservation. It is a popular ecotourism destination.
Nuvilly (Lang-frp IPA-frp) is a municipality in the district of Broye, in the canton of Fribourg, Switzerland.

Lü is a former municipality in the canton of Graubünden, Switzerland, located in the Val Müstair region. Situated at an elevation of 1,920 meters (6,300 ft), it is one of the highest villages in Europe. First mentioned in 1466, Lü covered an area of 6.8 km² and had a population of 63 as of 2008, with most residents speaking Rhaeto-Romance. The village is known for its scenic hiking trails and the Alpine Astrovillage Lü-Stailas, a public observatory inaugurated in 2009, offering favorable conditions for astronomy.

The Gryphon is a fictional character devised by Lewis Carroll in the popular 1865 book Alice's Adventures in Wonderland. True to the conventional view of a griffin, he has the head, talons, and wings of an eagle and the body of a lion.

Matthew Daniels (born September 27, 1989) is a former American football safety and current special teams coach of the Minnesota Vikings of the National Football League (NFL). He was signed by the St. Louis Rams as an undrafted free agent in 2012. He played college football for Duke.

KWJC (91.9 FM) is a non-commercial radio station licensed to Liberty, Missouri, serving the Kansas City metropolitan area with a classical music format. Owned by the University of Missouri-Kansas City (UMKC) and branded as "91.9 Classical KC," the station originally launched in 1974 as KWPB under the ownership of William Jewell College. Initially an educational station with student-led programming, it underwent several format changes over the decades, including alternative and adult contemporary music. In 2019, UMKC acquired KWJC for $2 million, relaunching it in 2020 as a full-time classical music station, restoring the format to Kansas City's FM airwaves.

The Epidemic Intelligence Service (EIS), established in 1951, is a program of the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) that trains "disease detectives" to investigate and respond to public health crises. Initially created to address biological warfare concerns during the Korean War, the EIS has since been involved in numerous domestic and international disease outbreaks, including anthrax, Ebola, and West Nile virus. EIS officers undergo rigorous training and are deployed to state, local, and international health agencies. The program has inspired similar Field Epidemiology Training Programs worldwide and is a key pathway into the Public Health Service Commissioned Corps.

A *gharana* is a system of lineage and stylistic tradition in Indian classical music and dance, rooted in the guru–shishya (teacher–student) tradition. It encompasses various forms, including khyal, dhrupad, thumri, tabla, and Kathak dance, each with distinct regional styles and techniques. The khyal gharanas, influenced by the decline of the Mughal Empire, developed in princely states like Gwalior, Lucknow, and Patiala, emphasizing unique approaches to improvisation and rhythm. Similarly, thumri and Kathak gharanas evolved with distinct characteristics, such as the Benares, Lucknow, and Jaipur-Atrauli styles, reflecting the rich diversity and adaptability of Indian classical traditions.

Pivotal (19 January 1993 – 19 November 2021) was a British Thoroughbred racehorse and influential sire. Bred and owned by Cheveley Park Stud, he was trained by Sir Mark Prescott and had a racing record of six starts, including major wins in the 1996 King's Stand Stakes and Nunthorpe Stakes. Retired to stud in 1997, Pivotal became a highly successful breeding stallion, siring numerous major winners. He was retired from stud duties in 2021 and died later that year.

Akumaa Mama Zimbi, also known professionally as Dr. Joyce Akumaa Dongotey-Padi, is a Ghanaian television and radio broadcast journalist, women's rights activist, blogger, marriage counselor, and an actress in Ghana. Akumaa is known for her fascinating, yet sexually stimulating, ways of expressing "Medaase" (Thank you). Her exceptional ways of wearing her headgear also comes off as a distinct Akumaa brand. Little is known about Akumaa's early life as well as her family and educational background. However, it is known that her father is a policeman and her husband is a businessman to whom she has been married for over twenty-four years

Panhard was a French motor vehicle manufacturer that began as one of the first makers of automobiles. It was a manufacturer of light tactical and military vehicles. Its final incarnation, now owned by Renault Trucks Defense, was formed by the acquisition of Panhard by Auverland in 2005, and then by Renault in 2012. In 2018 Renault Trucks Defense, ACMAT and Panhard combined under a single brand, Arquus.
HMS Modeste was a 64-gun third rate ship of the line of the Royal Navy. She was previously the 64-gun Modeste, of the French Navy, launched in 1759 and captured later that year.

Mark V. Campbell (born 1978) is a Canadian academic, disc jockey and writer. He was raised in Scarborough, a suburb of Toronto, Canada. Currently, he is an assistant professor and Associate Chair of the Department of Arts, Culture and Media, University of Toronto Scarborough. He is the founder of the Northside Hip Hop Archive, an online archive that digitizes oral histories, event flyers, posters and analog recordings that document the beginnings of Canadian hip hop. Campbell is the 2020-21 Jackman Humanities Institute UTSC Fellow and a Connaught Early Career Fellow at the University of Toronto. Campbell was formerly Director of FCAD Forum for Cultural Strategies and adjunct professor at the RTA School of Media, Ryerson University.

Campbell was a Banting postdoctoral fellow in the Department of Fine Arts at the University of Regina. He is a fellow and former postdoctoral fellow with the International Institute for Critical Studies in Improvisation. Campbell became a disc jockey in 1994 and co-hosted the Bigger than Hip-Hop Show on community radio from 1997 to 2015.

Campbell's work focuses on new modalities of being human, sonic innovations within Black music, and the knowledge production of digital archives.

The 1973 World Snooker Championship was a professional snooker tournament held from 16 to 28 April at the City Exhibition Halls in Manchester, England. Organized by the World Professional Billiards and Snooker Association (WPBSA) and sponsored by Park Drive, it featured a record 24 entrants and a total prize fund of £8,000, with £1,500 awarded to the winner. The tournament adopted a single-elimination format, with matches ranging from best-of-17 frames in the first round to best-of-75 frames in the final. 

Ray Reardon of Wales defeated Australia's Eddie Charlton 38–32 in the final to claim his second world title, having first won in 1970. Reardon staged a remarkable comeback after losing the first session 0–7, ultimately overcoming Charlton with his superior shot-making and flair. The event also saw John Spencer achieve the highest break of 139. For the first time, parts of the final were televised by the BBC, marking a significant moment in snooker's history. The tournament drew 25,000 spectators, exceeding expectations, and featured future world champions Cliff Thorburn and Dennis Taylor making their debuts. Reardon would go on to win four more world titles.

*End of the World* (French: *La Fin du monde*) is a 1931 French science fiction film directed by Abel Gance. The film follows the story of a scientist, Martial Novalic, who discovers that Lexell's Comet is on a collision course with Earth, leading to global chaos, societal collapse, and a desperate attempt to unite humanity under a single world government. The film explores themes of apocalypse, human greed, and redemption, culminating in the comet narrowly missing Earth after widespread destruction.

Gance had envisioned the project since 1913 and began production in 1929, originally intending for the film to run over three hours. However, the producers took control and heavily edited it down to 105 minutes, significantly altering Gance's vision. Upon its release in France in January 1931, the film was met with critical and commercial failure, with Gance himself calling it "an abortive work." A heavily re-edited version was later released in the United States in 1934 under the title *Paris After Dark*.

Elections to the Legislative Assembly of the Indian state of Mysore were held on 26 March 1952. 394 candidates contested for 99 seats in 80 constituencies in the Assembly. There were 19 two-member constituencies and 61 single-member constituencies, accounting for 99 seats.
Diana Nemiroff is a Canadian curator and writer known for groundbreaking exhibitions and receiving the 2012 Governor General's Award.

Giant skippers are medium-sized butterflies belonging to the subfamily Megathyminae within the family Hesperiidae. Known for their thick bodies and brown coloration with yellow markings, they are larger than other Hesperiidae species. Males are territorial, with fast flight and long hairlike scales on their upperwings. Unlike most butterflies, adults rarely feed on flowers, though males drink from wet sand. Their eggs are laid on or near host plants, and the caterpillars create silk-lined tunnels in leaves or stems, where they feed and pupate. The subfamily includes genera such as *Megathymus*, *Stallingsia*, and *Agathymus*.

The Reading Municipal Building, located at 49 Pleasant Street in Reading, Massachusetts, is a historic Renaissance Revival structure built in 1883. Designed by architect Horace G. Wadlin, it originally served as the town's first purpose-built municipal building, housing town offices, a fire station, and a jail. It remained a fire station until the mid-20th century and was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1984. It now serves as a senior center.

Barley yellow mosaic virus (BaYMV) is a plant pathogenic virus belonging to the genus *Bymovirus* in the family *Potyviridae*. It primarily infects barley (*Hordeum vulgare*), causing systemic symptoms such as yellowing, mosaic flecking, stunting, and necrosis, which can lead to significant yield losses. The virus is transmitted through the soil-borne protist *Polymyxa graminis* and spreads via zoospores that invade barley roots. BaYMV thrives in regions with cold winters and wet autumns, particularly in Eastern Asia and Western Europe, where it has caused yield losses of up to 80%. Management relies on resistant barley cultivars, as no cure exists for infected plants.
Oswald Chettle Mazengarb CBE QC (31 May 1890 – 27 November 1963), known as Ossie Mazengarb, was a New Zealand barrister.

The deliberate food shortage conspiracy theory claims that food shortages are intentionally caused by governments or other entities. These claims, often spread on social media, lack evidence and have been widely debunked by experts and fact-checking organizations.

Shanghai Street (Chinese: 上海街) is one of the oldest streets in Hong Kong, located in the Yau Ma Tei and Mong Kok districts of Kowloon. Originally called Station Street, it was renamed in 1909 to reflect Hong Kong's trade connections with Shanghai. The street, built on reclaimed land, was a major commercial hub from the late 19th to mid-20th century, featuring traditional Chinese shops and trades. Notable landmarks include the Engineer's Office of the Former Pumping Station, a Grade I historic building, and pre-war shophouses (tong lau), which are part of ongoing heritage conservation and revitalization efforts.
Kleiner Tiergarten is a historic urban park in Moabit, Berlin.

Christmas in Spiceworld Tour was the second concert tour by British girl group the Spice Girls. The eight-show tour was launched following "solo projects, marriages, motherhood and another round of slagging in the press", as a reunion for the girls.
Raymond Duchamp-Villon (5 November 1876 – 9 October 1918) was a French sculptor.

The 1973 World Snooker Championship (also known as 1973 Park Drive World Snooker Championship for sponsorship reasons) was a snooker tournament that took place from 16 to 28 April 1973 at the City Exhibition Halls in Manchester, England. The scheduling was a change of practice from championships in the preceding years, which had taken place over several months. The tournament was the 1973 edition of the World Snooker Championship established in 1927. The 1973 tournament was promoted by Peter West and Patrick Nally, and sponsored by tobacco brand Park Drive, with £8,000 prize money. There were 24 entrants, a new championship record.

Ray Reardon won the title by defeating Eddie Charlton 38 frames to 32 in the final. It was Reardon's second World Championship win, after his first in 1970. He went on to win a further four titles, the last of them in 1978. Defending champion Alex Higgins lost 9–13 to Charlton in the semi-finals. Higgins's quarter-final match against Fred Davis was interrupted when rainwater leaked onto the snooker table they were using. There were six century breaks during the competition, the highest of which was 139 by John Spencer.

*Road Rules: Down Under* is the sixth season of MTV's reality television series *Road Rules*. It aired from June 22 to September 7, 1998, and featured cast members Chadwick Pelletier, Christina Pazsitzky, Kefla Hare, Piggy Thomas, Shayne McBride, and Susie Meister. The season consisted of 13 episodes.

John Thomas Gordon Ruddy (born 24 October 1986) is an English professional footballer who plays as a goalkeeper for Birmingham City. Beginning his career at Cambridge United, Ruddy made his professional debut in 2004 before joining Everton in 2005. During his time at Everton, he had numerous loan spells, including at Motherwell, where he impressed with 15 clean sheets in the 2009–10 season. In 2010, Ruddy signed for Norwich City, where he became a key player, helping the team achieve promotion to the Premier League and earning the club's Player's Player of the Season award in 2012. 

Ruddy later joined Wolverhampton Wanderers in 2017, playing a pivotal role in their promotion to the Premier League and earning the EFL Golden Glove Award. After five years with Wolves, he moved to Birmingham City in 2022, where he remains a regular starter. Internationally, Ruddy earned one cap for England in 2012. Known for his consistency and leadership, he has enjoyed a long and successful career in English football.
Todd Merlin Compton (born 1952) is an American historian in the fields of Mormon history and classics. Compton is a respected authority on the plural wives of the LDS Church founder, Joseph Smith.
Roger Shah (IPA-de; born 29 November 1972), also known as DJ Shah and Sunlounger, is a German electronic music composer and producer.
John Patrick (born February 29, 1968) is an American professional basketball coach and former professional basketball player.

Panhard was a French automobile and military vehicle manufacturer founded in 1887 by René Panhard and Émile Levassor. Initially known as Panhard et Levassor, the company pioneered automotive innovations, including the modern transmission and the standard car layout. It produced passenger cars until 1967, after which it focused solely on military vehicles. Acquired by Citroën and later Renault Trucks Defense, Panhard ceased operations in 2018. Its legacy includes the Panhard rod suspension system.
Pelican Waters is a suburb of Caloundra in the Sunshine Coast Region, Queensland, Australia, established in 1995. Known for its canal estates, it had a population of 6,277 in the 2016 census.

The Raccoon River is a 30.8-mile-long (49.6 km) tributary of the Des Moines River in central Iowa, United States, with its length extending to 226 miles (364 km) when including its longest fork. Originating in an intensely cultivated agricultural region, the river flows eastward through Dallas and Polk counties, joining the Des Moines River near downtown Des Moines. It has historically provided drinking water to the Des Moines metropolitan area, though it has faced challenges such as flooding during the Great Flood of 1993, which disrupted the city's water supply.

The river is also impacted by agricultural pollution, with nitrate runoff from fertilizers causing periodic spikes in contamination levels, exceeding safe drinking water standards. First documented on an 1814 map by Lewis and Clark, the Raccoon River remains a vital waterway in Iowa's landscape and history.

End of the World (Lang-fr) is a 1931 French science fiction film directed by Abel Gance based on the novel Omega: The Last Days of the World by Camille Flammarion. The film stars Victor Francen as Martial Novalic, Colette Darfeuil as Genevieve de Murcie, Abel Gance as Jean Novalic, and Jeanne Brindau as Madame Novalic. The plot concerns a comet hurtling toward Earth on a collision course and the different reactions people have to the impending disaster. Scientist Martial Novalic who discovers the comet, seeks a solution to the problem and becomes a fugitive after skeptical authorities blame him for starting a mass panic.

End of the World was director Abel Gance's first sound film. The original film was to be over three hours long, but the backing production took the film from Gance, and cut it to be 105 minutes. It was again cut on its release in the United States under the title of Paris after Dark. Neither abridged version of the film was well received by audiences or critics.

In medicine, an avulsion is an injury in which a body structure is torn off by either trauma or surgery (from the Latin avellere, meaning "to tear off"). The term most commonly refers to a surface trauma where all layers of the skin have been torn away, exposing the underlying structures (i.e., subcutaneous tissue, muscle, tendons, or bone). This is similar to an abrasion but more severe, as body parts such as an eyelid or an ear can be partially or fully detached from the body.

Vyacheslav Viktorovich Yaikov (born March 1, 1974), known as "The Kopeysk Strangler," is a Russian serial killer who murdered six women in Chelyabinsk between 2002 and 2003. Diagnosed with an intellectual disability, he was found insane at the time of the crimes and sentenced to compulsory psychiatric treatment.

The Epidemic Intelligence Service (EIS) is a program of the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). The modern EIS is a two-year, hands-on post-doctoral training program in epidemiology, with a focus on field work.

Messier 83 or M83, also known as the Southern Pinwheel Galaxy and NGC 5236, is a barred spiral galaxy approximately 15 million light-years away in the constellation borders of Hydra and Centaurus. Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille discovered M83 on 23 February 1752 at the Cape of Good Hope. Charles Messier added it to his catalogue of nebulous objects (now known as the Messier Catalogue) in March 1781. It is one of the closest and brightest barred spiral galaxies in the sky, and is visible with binoculars. Its nickname of the Southern Pinwheel derives from its resemblance to the Pinwheel Galaxy (M101).

The 1912–13 season was Blackpool F.C.'s 16th season (13th consecutive) in the Football League. They competed in the twenty-team Division Two, then the second tier of English football, finishing bottom. Their application for re-election was successful.

George Wilson was the club's top scorer, with ten goals.

"Bizarre Love Triangle" is a 1986 single by the English band New Order, featured on their fourth studio album, *Brotherhood*. Written by band members Gillian Gilbert, Peter Hook, Stephen Morris, and Bernard Sumner, the song is a hallmark of the new wave, synth-pop, and alternative dance genres. The single, released on November 5, 1986, gained acclaim for its innovative use of electronic instrumentation, including the Fairlight CMI music workstation, and its layered production. A remixed 12-inch version by Shep Pettibone appears on the compilation *Substance*, while other versions feature on subsequent compilations like *Best Of* and *Singles*.

The music video, directed by Robert Longo, incorporates imagery inspired by his "Men in the Cities" lithographs and features surreal scenes of people in business suits flying through the air. Critically lauded as a "synth-pop masterpiece," the song has been ranked among the greatest of all time by *Rolling Stone*, *Billboard*, and other publications, cementing its legacy in New Order's discography.

*The Geologist* is a painting by German artist Carl Spitzweg, created around 1860 using glue paint on paper mounted on canvas. It depicts a geologist examining rocks in a sunlit, rocky landscape. The painting is part of the collection at the Von der Heydt Museum in Wuppertal, Germany. The work reflects Spitzweg's interest in natural sciences and features detailed, earthy tones. The painting's provenance traces back to New York in 1863 before entering the Von der Heydt Museum through Fritz Reimann's legacy. Spitzweg created at least three versions of *The Geologist*, with other versions held at the Städtische Galerie Pforzheim and the Museum Georg Schäfer.

Henry I of Cyprus (3 May 1217 – 18 January 1253) was King of Cyprus from 1218 until his death. Crowned at the age of eight, his early reign was marked by political struggles, including the intervention of Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor, and the subsequent War of the Lombards. Henry assumed full control of the kingdom in 1232 and maintained strong ties with the Ibelin family. He also served as Regent of Jerusalem from 1246 to 1253. Henry was married three times and was succeeded by his son, Hugh II.
Kisnána is a village in Heves county, Hungary, located 25 kilometres (16 mi) from Gyöngyös to the northeast. There is a castle in the village that was built in the 11th or 12th century.

Miller Thomson LLP is one of Canada's largest national law firms, founded on July 1, 1957, in Toronto, Ontario. With 12 offices across the country and over 550 lawyers, the firm provides expertise in a wide range of practice areas, including corporate law, real estate, intellectual property, and litigation. Known for its strategic mergers and affiliations, Miller Thomson is consistently ranked among Canada’s leading law firms and is a member of the global legal network Multilaw.

Ham Green Pottery refers to medieval ceramics produced near the River Avon in England, primarily between the 12th and 13th centuries. Known for its distinctive lead-glazed jugs, the pottery featured decorative patterns, including floral designs and figures. Production ceased by 1275, and fragments have been uncovered in archaeological excavations, serving as valuable dating tools. The pottery was widely traded across Britain, particularly in South West England, South Wales, and Ireland. Artifacts are preserved in museums, including Bristol's M Shed.

GAL (born 1940 in Auderghem, Brussels) is a Belgian cartoonist and illustrator known for his socially and politically engaged work. Active since 1960, his cartoons have appeared in prominent publications like *Knack* and *De Zwijger*. His distinctive style blends artistic techniques with sharp political commentary, often addressing issues like apartheid and far-right politics. GAL has contributed to numerous social causes, including Oxfam and Amnesty International, and his work has been featured globally. In 2019, he received an honorary doctorate from the Free University in Brussels.
Scott Campbell (born December 28, 1973), known professionally as Scott C., is an American artist and production designer, known for his work for LucasArts and Double Fine Productions.

Léonie d'Aunet (2 July 1820 – 3 March 1879) was a French author, playwright, and travel writer, known for being the first woman to participate in a scientific Arctic expedition. She gained recognition for her travelogue *Voyage d'une femme au Spitzberg* and her novels, plays, and journalistic contributions. D'Aunet's personal life included a notable liaison with Victor Hugo, which inspired his poetry but also led to public scandal.

The *Cello Concerto* by Esa-Pekka Salonen is a 2017 composition for solo cello and orchestra, incorporating live electronics. Written for cellist Yo-Yo Ma, the concerto is structured in three movements and explores themes of cosmic imagery and opposing forces, blending lyrical passages with dynamic, dance-like sections. Praised for its innovative orchestration and technical demands, the work has received mixed critical reception, with some highlighting its creativity and others questioning its emotional depth.
Lincoln is an unincorporated community and census-designated place (CDP) in Lewis and Clark County, Montana, United States. As of the 2010 census, the population was 1,013.

The **Stone–Čech compactification** is a construction in topology that associates a compact Hausdorff space, denoted βX, to a given topological space X. It is characterized by a universal property: any continuous map from X to a compact Hausdorff space uniquely extends to a continuous map from βX. This makes βX the largest and most "complete" compactification of X, with the image of X being dense in βX. The construction is particularly significant for Tychonoff spaces, where it preserves the topological properties of X.

The concept was developed independently by Andrey Tikhonov, Eduard Čech, and Marshall Stone in the 1930s. Tikhonov introduced completely regular spaces and proved the existence of compactifications, while Čech and Stone provided further constructions and formalized the notation βX.

The Stone–Čech compactification can be constructed using various methods, including products of compact spaces, ultrafilters, and C*-algebras. It has applications in set-theoretic topology, functional analysis, and algebra, such as characterizing bounded sequences and extending operations on semigroups. A notable example is βN, the compactification of the natural numbers, which has been extensively studied for its complex structure and connections to set theory.

St. James Cemetery, established in 1844, is a historic non-denominational cemetery located at 635 Parliament Street in Toronto, Ontario, Canada. Owned by the Cathedral Church of St. James, it was created to address the lack of burial space around the cathedral. The cemetery features the Chapel of St. James-the-Less, a Victorian Gothic structure built in 1860–1861 and designated a National Historic Site of Canada in 1990. It also contains 42 Commonwealth war graves and has conducted over 95,000 interments and 114,000 cremations.

Pakistan has bilateral and multilateral trade agreements with many nations and international organizations. It is a member of the World Trade Organization, part of the South Asian Free Trade Area agreement and the China–Pakistan Free Trade Agreement. Fluctuating world demand for its exports, domestic political uncertainty, and the impact of occasional droughts on its agricultural production have all contributed to variability in Pakistan's trade deficit. The trade deficit for the fiscal year 2013/14 is $7.743 billion, exports are $10.367 billion in July–November 2013 and imports are $18.110 billion.

Pakistan's exports continue to be dominated by manpower export in the subcontinent, cotton textiles and apparel. Imports include petroleum and petroleum products, chemicals, fertilizer, capital goods, industrial raw materials, and consumer products.

On 12 December 2013, the European Union granted GSP Plus status to Pakistan until 2017, which enabled it to export 20% of its good with 0 tariff and 70 percent at preferential rates to the EU market. This status was given after the European Parliament passed the resolution by 406-186 votes.

Poilão is a small uninhabited island in the Bijagós Archipelago of Guinea-Bissau. It contains the country's southernmost point, at 10°51'53"N,15°43'36"W. It is part of the João Vieira and Poilão Marine National Park. The island is heavily forested. The nearest islands are Meio, 7 nmi (13 km) to the north, Cavalos, 9 nmi (17 km) to the north and João Vieira, 11 nmi (20 km) to the north-northeast. Its area is 43 ha. Between ca. 7000 and 29,000 green sea turtle (Chelonia mydas) nests are laid per year at the globally important site of Poilão Island. There is a lighthouse on the island, with focal height 27 m (89 ft).

Henry I of Cyprus, nicknamed the Fat (Lang-fr; 3 May 1217 – 18 January 1253 at Nicosia) was King of Cyprus from 1218 to 1253. He was the son of Hugh I of Cyprus and Alice of Champagne. When his father Hugh I died on January 10, 1218, the 8-month-old Henry became king. His mother was the official regent, but handed off the actual governing to her uncle, Philip of Ibelin. When Philip died, the effective regency passed to his brother, John of Ibelin, the Old Lord of Beirut.

*Oxalis latifolia*, commonly known as broadleaf woodsorrel, is a perennial herb in the family Oxalidaceae. Native to the Americas, it spreads via bulbs and stolons and is considered an invasive species globally.

The Western Sahara peace process refers to the international efforts to resolve the Western Sahara conflict. The conflict has failed so far to result in permanent peace between Morocco and the Polisario Front (including its self declared Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic). The standing issues of the peace process include Sahrawi refugees (specifically Sahrawi refugee camps in Algeria), and human rights in Western Sahara.

Lü is a village in the Val Müstair municipality in the district of Inn in the Swiss canton of Graubünden. In 2009 Lü merged with Fuldera, Müstair, Santa Maria Val Müstair, Tschierv and Valchava to form the municipality of Val Müstair.

The Stockade Building System was a construction method developed by Buckminster Fuller and James R. Hewlett in the 1920s. It utilized fibrous blocks coated with plaster, designed to be lightweight, non-absorbent, and easily modified. Patented in 1927, the system underwent field trials in Florida.

Bilston is a small village in Midlothian, Scotland. It is located on the edge of Edinburgh, just south of Loanhead on the A701. The Bilston Burn Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) was occupied from 2002 until the mid-2010s by protestors who successfully opposed plans for a bypass.

*Poot Mae Nam Khong* is a Thai television drama series that aired in three iterations: 1992–1993, 2008, and 2022. Created by Sumruay Rukchart, the series follows Dr. Akkanee, who uncovers a vengeful spirit tied to the Mekong River and his past life. Combining romance, supernatural elements, and themes of reincarnation, the show explores love, betrayal, and redemption. It gained significant popularity, particularly during its 2008 run, becoming a ratings success and revitalizing the career of lead actress Jui Warattaya.
al-Majd (Lang-ar) is a Palestinian village located eighteen kilometers south-west of Hebron. The village is in the Hebron Governorate Southern West Bank.
Auguste-Henri-Victor Grandjean de Montigny (15 July 1776 – 2 March 1850) was a French architect who had considerable influence on the development of architecture in Brazil.

The Dutch frigate *Proserpine* was launched in 1801 for the Batavian Republic and captured by the British in 1804 during the capture of Surinam. Renamed HMS *Amsterdam*, it served as a storeship and guardship before being sold in 1815. It had a burthen of 849 tons and carried 24-pounder carronades.

Following the termination of hostilities in World War II, the Allies were in control of the defeated Axis countries. Anticipating the defeat of Germany and Japan, they had already set up the European Advisory Commission and a proposed Far Eastern Advisory Commission to make recommendations for the post war period. Accordingly, they managed their control of the defeated countries through Allied Commissions, often referred to as Allied Control Commissions (ACC), consisting of representatives of the major Allies.

RDE-1 (RNAi-DEfective 1) is a primary Argonaute protein required for RNA-mediated interference (RNAi) in Caenorhabditis elegans. The rde-1 gene locus was first characterized in C. elegans mutants resistant to RNAi, and is a member of a highly conserved Piwi gene family that includes plant, Drosophila, and vertebrate homologs.
*Odostomia farallonensis* is a species of small sea snail in the family Pyramidellidae. It is found in the Pacific Ocean near California's Farallon Islands.

Messier 83 (M83), also known as the Southern Pinwheel Galaxy, is a barred spiral galaxy located in the constellation Hydra, approximately 14.7 million light-years from Earth. Classified as SAB(s)c, it features loosely wound spiral arms and a weak central bar. M83 is notable for its high star formation rate, particularly along the leading edges of its spiral arms, and the discovery of new stars in its outer regions, as reported by NASA in 2008. It has hosted six observed supernovae and is a central member of the Centaurus A/M83 Group, a nearby galaxy group.

Joseph Henry Rodriguez (born December 12, 1930) is a Senior United States District Judge for the District of New Jersey. Appointed by President Ronald Reagan in 1985, he served as an active judge until assuming senior status in 1998. Prior to his judicial career, Rodriguez was the Public Advocate of New Jersey from 1982 to 1985 and held leadership roles in state education and investigative commissions. A graduate of La Salle University and Rutgers School of Law–Camden, he also practiced law privately in Camden, New Jersey, for over two decades.

Meri (Hebrew: מר"י) was a left-wing political party in Israel founded in the 1960s by Uri Avnery, editor of the HaOlam HaZeh news magazine. Initially successful in elections, it later dissolved in the 1970s, merging into the Left Camp of Israel.

RPT Realty is a publicly traded real estate investment trust (REIT) specializing in open-air shopping centers. Founded in 1955 by Aaron and William Gershenson as A & W Management Company, the company transitioned to public ownership in 1996 through a reverse merger with RPS Realty Trust, adopting the name Ramco-Gershenson Properties Trust. It was later rebranded as RPT Realty in 2018. Headquartered in New York City, the company is a component of the S&P 600 and reported $234 million in revenue and $91 million in net income in 2019. RPT Realty manages assets totaling $1.918 billion with 104 employees.

Charles Boarman (December 24, 1795 – September 13, 1879) was a United States Navy officer who served for over 68 years, making him one of the longest-serving officers in U.S. Navy history. Born in Bryantown, Maryland, into a prominent colonial Maryland family, Boarman joined the Navy in 1811 and served during the War of 1812 aboard the USS Jefferson. Over his extensive career, he commanded several ships, including the USS Weasel, USS Java, and USS Brandywine, and held significant posts such as Commandant of the Brooklyn Navy Yard. He participated in anti-piracy operations in the West Indies, served in the Mediterranean and Brazil Squadrons, and oversaw preparations for Commodore Matthew C. Perry's Japan expedition.

During the Civil War, despite his Southern roots, Boarman remained loyal to the Union and supported the creation of West Virginia. He retired as a rear admiral in 1876. Boarman and his wife, Mary Ann Abell, raised 13 children, and their family home in Martinsburg, West Virginia, known as the Boarman House, is a historic landmark. Boarman died in Martinsburg in 1879 and was buried in Saint Joseph's Cemetery. His legacy includes his contributions to naval service and his family's prominence in American history, with descendants who became pioneers, physicians, and musicians.

Uzel Holding was a Turkish agricultural machinery and automotive company founded in 1937 by Ibrahim Uzel in Bursa, Turkey. It grew from producing leaf springs to becoming one of the world's largest tractor manufacturers, with operations in over 75 countries and notable acquisitions, including Gebrüder Holder GmbH and a stake in the Ursus Tractor Factory. Despite its global success, financial difficulties and internal disputes led to its collapse in 2011 and bankruptcy in 2012.

Saint Melchior, or Melichior, was purportedly one of the Biblical Magi along with Caspar and Balthazar who visited the infant Jesus after he was born. Melchior was often referred to as the oldest member of the Magi. He was traditionally called the King of Persia and brought the gift of gold to Jesus. In the Western Christian church, he is regarded as a saint (as are the other two Magi).

The alternative Christmas message is an annual broadcast by Channel 4, beginning in 1993, featuring contemporary or controversial figures delivering a short message as a counterpoint to the Queen's Christmas message.

In mathematics, Betti numbers are fundamental topological invariants that describe the number of "holes" in a topological space across different dimensions. Informally, the kth Betti number, \( b_k \), represents the number of k-dimensional holes, such as connected components (\( b_0 \)), loops (\( b_1 \)), or voids (\( b_2 \)). For example, a torus has \( b_0 = 1 \), \( b_1 = 2 \), and \( b_2 = 1 \), corresponding to one connected component, two circular holes, and one enclosed cavity.

Formally, Betti numbers are defined as the rank of the kth homology group, \( H_k(X) \), of a space \( X \), or equivalently, the dimension of \( H_k(X; F) \) as a vector space over a field \( F \). They are closely related to the Poincaré polynomial, which encodes the Betti numbers as coefficients in a generating function.

Betti numbers have applications in graph theory, simplicial complexes, and differential geometry, where they relate to the dimensions of spaces of differential forms via de Rham's theorem and Hodge theory. They also play a role in Morse theory and the study of infinite-dimensional spaces.
Stevan Mirković (Serbian Cyrillic: Стеван Мирковић; 27 October 1927 – 26 September 2015) was a Serb general of the Yugoslav People's Army (JNA).
John Doyley (1602–1660) was an English politician who sat in the House of Commons from 1646 to 1648.

Vahida Maglajlić (17 April 1907 – 1 April 1943) was a Yugoslav Partisan recognized as a People's Hero of Yugoslavia for her part in the struggle against the Axis powers during World War II. She was the only Bosnian Muslim woman to receive the order.

Born to a Banja Luka qadi, Maglajlić was denied a higher education by her father. She took up various social and humanitarian causes, including women's rights. After Yugoslavia was invaded by the Axis powers in April 1941, Bosnia became part of the Independent State of Croatia, a fascist puppet state. Maglajlić entered the Communist Party of Yugoslavia in May and joined the growing Partisan resistance movement. Her high social status allowed her to inconspicuously shelter and equip local Partisans for several months, but she was eventually discovered by the authorities and sent to prison. After months of torture, Maglajlić escaped and joined the Partisans. She was an influential political figure in Bosanska Krajina until her death in combat with German troops in Mala Krupska Rujiška.
